title: Get Started with facet
text: facet package provides unified framework Generalizability Theory (G-Theory). quick start, walk standard analysis: conducting G-study, running D-study.

previous_headings: Introduction
title: Get Started with facet
text: First, load facet one built-datasets, brennan. Reading data. brennan dataset one row per observation: 10 persons × 3 tasks × 4 raters per task = 120 rows. row records one rater’s score one person one task. nested design: four raters rate every task; rather, task panel raters (Rater:Task right way write random effect, just Rater). Score column observed rating; three columns identify cell design row belongs .
code: library(facet) data(brennan) head(brennan) #>   Task Person Rater Score y #> 1    1      1     1     5 5 #> 2    1      2     1     9 9 #> 3    1      3     1     3 3 #> 4    1      4     1     7 7 #> 5    1      5     1     9 9 #> 6    1      6     1     3 3

previous_headings: Introduction
title: Get Started with facet
text: Use gstudy() function estimate variance components. default, facet uses lme4 (Restricted Maximum Likelihood) hood. Interpreting variance components. output shows four variance components: Person (universe score variance — true differences persons), Task (differences average difficulty across tasks), Rater:Task (differences leniency/severity raters within task), Person:Task (interaction — whether person performs better tasks others). “pct” column shows fraction total variance component accounts . healthy measurement large Person component (typically >30%) small Person:Task interaction (test fair across tasks). facet taking large fraction variance, facet invest levels (tasks, raters) next measurement campaign.
code: g_obj <- gstudy(Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) +   (1 | Person:Task),   data = brennan)  # View the variance components print(g_obj) #> Generalizability Study (G-Study) #> ================================ #>  #> Backend: lme4  #> Formula: Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) + (1 | Person:Task)  #> Number of observations: 120  #> Multivariate: No  #>  #> Object of measurement: Person  #> Facets: Person, Task, Rater  #>  #> Sample Sizes: #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   effect             type            n #>   <chr>              <chr>       <dbl> #> 1 Person             main           10 #> 2 Task               main            3 #> 3 Rater:Task         interaction    12 #> 4 Person:Task        interaction    30 #> 5 Person:Rater (res) residual      120 #> 6 Rater per Task     nested          4 #>  #> Nested details: #>   Rater per Task: 3 Tasks, 4.0 Raters per Task #>  #> Variance Components: #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>   component   estimate   pct #>   <chr>          <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 Person         0.473 10.8  #> 2 Task           0.325  7.42 #> 3 Rater:Task     0.647 14.8  #> 4 Person:Task    0.56  12.8  #> 5 Residual       2.38  54.3

previous_headings: Introduction
title: Get Started with facet
text: Plotting random effects helps identify facets contribute variance.  Reading variance plot. bar represents variance component, height shows magnitude (variance proportion scale depending type argument). useful patterns look : (1) tall Person bar means instrument good distinguishing individuals; (2) tall facet bar (e.g., Task Rater:Task) means facet major source error add levels ; (3) similar heights across components suggest balanced design single facet dominates. Compare bar heights “pct” column step 2 confirm relative contributions.
code: plot(g_obj)

previous_headings: Introduction
title: Get Started with facet
text: Decision (D) study calculates dependability measurement design. can project changing sample sizes facets impacts reliability. Interpreting D-study output. Two coefficients reported: G (generalizability coefficient, relative decisions like “rank persons”) Phi (dependability coefficient, absolute decisions like “person meet standard?”). Conventional thresholds: G ≥ 0.80 high-stakes decisions (selection, certification), G ≥ 0.70 medium-stakes (formative feedback, progress monitoring), G ≥ 0.60 low-stakes research. Phi always ≤ G; gap tells much absolute-error variance (e.g., task-difficulty shifts) contributing. projected G target, next step run sweep — vary n across multiple values find smallest design achieves reliability need.
code: # Project a design with 3 Tasks and 4 Raters d_obj <- dstudy(g_obj, n = list(Task = 3, Rater = 4))  print(d_obj) #> Decision Study (D-Study) #> ======================== #>  #> Based on G-Study with lme4 backend #> Object of measurement: Person  #> Universe components: Person  #> Error components for relative error (sigma2_delta): Person:Task, Person:Rater (Residual)  #> Error components for absolute error (sigma2_delta_abs): Task, Rater:Task, Person:Task, Person:Rater (Residual)  #>  #> Sample Sizes: #>  Task: 3 #>  Rater: 4 #>  #> Variance Components: #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>   component   dim   var_unscaled pct_unscaled var_scaled pct_scaled #>   <chr>       <chr>        <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 Person      Score        0.473        10.8       0.473      33.4  #> 2 Task        Score        0.325         7.41      0.108       7.65 #> 3 Rater:Task  Score        0.647        14.8       0.054       3.80 #> 4 Person:Task Score        0.56         12.8       0.187      13.2  #> 5 Residual    Score        2.38         54.3       0.595      42.0  #>  #> Coefficients: #>    dim   uni sigma2_delta sigma2_delta_abs     g   phi #>  Score 0.473        0.782            0.944 0.377 0.334

title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Generalizability Theory (G-theory) comprehensive framework understanding reliability measurements. Developed Cronbach colleagues (Cronbach et al., 1963, 1972; Cronbach, Gleser, Nanda, & Rajaratnam, 1972), G-theory extends Classical Test Theory (CTT) recognizing measurement error monolithic arises multiple sources. allows researchers examine different facets measurement design—items, raters, tasks, occasions—contribute measurement error, optimize future measurement procedures accordingly. facet package provides comprehensive tools conducting G-theory analyses using variance component models. supports three estimation backends: frequentist approaches using lme4 (Restricted Maximum Likelihood) mom (Method Moments/ANOVA-based), Bayesian estimation using brms (-U-Turn Sampler/Hamiltonian Monte Carlo via Stan). package provides unified interface univariate multivariate designs, full support G-studies, D-studies, coefficient calculations uncertainty quantification.

previous_headings: Introduction to Generalizability Theory > Historical Context and Foundations
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: theoretical foundations generalizability theory established series landmark publications: Cronbach, Rajaratnam, & Gleser (1963) introduced core concept “universes admissible observations” established distinction relative absolute error, providing theoretical framework unified various approaches reliability estimation. paper liberated reliability theory constraints parallel forms, recognizing measurement generalized across multiple facets observation. Cronbach, Gleser, Nanda, & Rajaratnam (1972) provided definitive treatment G-theory seminal book Dependability Behavioral Measurements. work extended G-theory multivariate designs, introducing variance-covariance matrix estimation multiple dimensions establishing mathematical framework profile reliability composite score generalizability. Rajaratnam, Cronbach, & Gleser (1965) extended framework stratified-parallel tests, developing nested facet notation (e.g., items nested within subtests) remains standard G-theory notation today. Brennan (2001) synthesized developments Generalizability Theory, modern comprehensive treatment covers unbalanced designs, multivariate extensions, computational methods. text remains definitive reference G-theory methodology. Shavelson & Webb (1991, 2006) made G-theory accessible applied researchers primer Generalizability Theory: Primer, introduced generations researchers G-theory concepts clear explanations practical examples.

previous_headings: Introduction to Generalizability Theory > Historical Context and Foundations
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: computational implementation G-theory evolved several generations: Early Methods (1960s-1970s): Initial G-theory analyses relied hand calculations general-purpose ANOVA programs (BMDP, SPSS, SAS), limiting applications balanced designs. Researchers compute variance components manually expected mean squares. GENOVA (1983): Brennan’s GENOVA program first dedicated G-theory software, implementing ANOVA-based variance component estimation crossed nested designs. made G-theory practical first time, though required balanced data. urGENOVA mGENOVA (2001): Brennan extended software suite handle unbalanced designs (urGENOVA) multivariate analyses (mGENOVA), significantly expanding scope practical G-theory applications. William P. Vispoel made seminal contributions G-theory accessibility comparative software reviews (Vispoel, 1998, 2000), evaluating programs like GENOVA providing practical guidance researchers selecting appropriate computational tools. Vispoel also advanced multivariate G-theory applications personality assessment (Vispoel & Cook, 1994), demonstrating G-theory can separate multiple sources variance multi-trait, multi-method designs. G_String provides modern graphical interface built around urGENOVA, making G-theory accessible without command-line expertise (Bloch & Norman, 2012). Java-based tool particularly valuable medical education contexts. Modern R Packages: Contemporary implementations integrate G-theory mixed-effects modeling frameworks. facet package continues tradition providing R implementation three estimation backends (ANOVA/MOM, REML, Bayesian) full support univariate multivariate designs, leveraging computational efficiency modern tools like lme4 Stan.

previous_headings: Introduction to Generalizability Theory > Historical Context and Foundations
what: From Classical Test Theory to Generalizability Theory
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Classical Test Theory decomposes observed score XX true score TT measurement error ee: X=T+eX = T + e reliability coefficient CTT, typically Cronbach’s alpha, represents proportion observed score variance attributable true score variance. However, approach treats measurement error undifferentiated—distinguish error due different sources (e.g., items vs. raters) inform decisions improve measurement precision. G-theory addresses limitations partitioning observed score variance components attributable different facets measurement. Instead single reliability coefficient, G-theory provides: Variance components quantifying contribution facet total variance Generalizability (G) coefficients relative decisions (rank-ordering objects) Dependability (Phi) coefficients absolute decisions (comparing standards)

previous_headings: Introduction to Generalizability Theory > Historical Context and Foundations
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Facets sources measurement error design. Common facets include: Items: Questions tasks administered Raters: Judges observers scoring responses Tasks: Different performance activities Occasions: Times measurement Forms: Parallel versions instrument object measurement seek measure—typically persons (individuals, students, patients, etc.). distinction facets object crucial: facets sources error seek control, object target inference. universe admissible observations defines set acceptable measurement conditions. G-study estimates variance components sample observations; D-study uses estimates project reliability different measurement designs within universe. Variance components estimated mixed-effects model. simple person ×\times item design: Xpi=μ+μp+μi+μpi+epiX_{pi} = \mu + \mu_p + \mu_i + \mu_{pi} + e_{pi} : μ\mu grand mean μp\mu_p effect person pp μi\mu_i effect item ii μpi\mu_{pi} person ×\times item interaction epie_{pi} residual error effect associated variance component: σp2\sigma^2_p, σi2\sigma^2_i, σpi2\sigma^2_{pi}, σe2\sigma^2_e. G-studies (generalizability studies) estimate variance components observed data. goal understand much variance attributable facet. D-studies (decision studies) use G-study variance components project reliability different measurement designs. varying number levels facet (e.g., number items, raters), researchers can identify designs achieve desired reliability levels efficiently.

previous_headings: Introduction to Generalizability Theory > Univariate Generalizability Theory
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: G-theory distinguishes two types measurement error: Relative error (δ\delta) affects rank-ordering persons. crossed design: σδ2=στα2ni+σe2ni\sigma^2_\delta = \frac{\sigma^2_{\tau\alpha}}{n_i} + \frac{\sigma^2_e}{n_i} Relative error includes components involve object measurement—variance causes persons’ scores shift relative . Absolute error (Δ\Delta) affects absolute magnitude scores: σΔ2=σα2ni+στα2ni+σe2ni\sigma^2_\Delta = \frac{\sigma^2_\alpha}{n_i} + \frac{\sigma^2_{\tau\alpha}}{n_i} + \frac{\sigma^2_e}{n_i} Absolute error includes facets except object, systematic facet effect shifts observed mean away true mean.

previous_headings: Introduction to Generalizability Theory > Univariate Generalizability Theory
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: generalizability coefficient (G coefficient, Eρ2E\rho^2) quantifies reliability relative decisions: Eρ2=στ2στ2+σδ2E\rho^2 = \frac{\sigma^2_\tau}{\sigma^2_\tau + \sigma^2_\delta} dependability coefficient (Phi coefficient, Φ\Phi) quantifies reliability absolute decisions: Φ=στ2στ2+σΔ2\Phi = \frac{\sigma^2_\tau}{\sigma^2_\tau + \sigma^2_\Delta} coefficients range 0 1, higher values indicating greater reliability. Phi coefficient always less equal G coefficient absolute error includes sources variance.

title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: extension G-theory multiple dimensions first developed Cronbach, Gleser, Nanda, & Rajaratnam (1972), introduced variance-covariance matrices random effects across dimensions. foundational work established mathematical framework profile reliability composite score generalizability. Webb & Shavelson (1981) demonstrated practical applications multivariate G-theory educational assessment seminal paper showed multiple correlated dependent variables analyzed simultaneously. work made multivariate G-theory accessible applied researchers. Joe & Woodward (1976) established theoretical connections multivariate G-theory canonical correlation analysis, demonstrating multivariate generalizability equals squared canonical correlation certain conditions. Marcoulides (1990, 1994, 1996) made significant contributions multivariate G-theory methodology, developing alternative estimation approaches including SEM-based methods examining weighting schemes composite reliability. work addressed critical question weight dimensions forming composites. Brennan, Kim, & Lee (2022) extended multivariate G-theory handle complex design structures different random-effects structures exist level fixed facet, addressing limitations traditional multivariate approaches. Jiang, Raymond, Shi, & DiStefano (2020) provided modern R implementation multivariate G-theory using linear mixed-effects model frameworks, bridging G-theory contemporary mixed model methodology. Vispoel, Lee, Hong, & Chen (2023) provided comprehensive modern treatment multivariate G-theory psychological assessment contexts, demonstrating applications personality measures providing worked examples real data.

title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: multivariate design DD dimensions, model becomes: 𝐗pi=𝛍+𝛕p+𝛂i+(𝛕𝛂)pi+𝐞pi\mathbf{X}_{pi} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \boldsymbol{\tau}_p + \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i + (\boldsymbol{\tau\alpha})_{pi} + \mathbf{e}_{pi} bold notation indicates vectors length DD (one element per dimension). key extension variance components become variance-covariance matrices: 𝚺τ=(στ12στ1τ2στ1τ2στ22)\boldsymbol{\Sigma}_\tau = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma^2_{\tau_1} & \sigma_{\tau_1 \tau_2} \\ \sigma_{\tau_1 \tau_2} & \sigma^2_{\tau_2} \end{pmatrix} -diagonal elements represent covariances dimensions, quantifying extent random effects correlated across dimensions.

code: # Wide format: Task1, Task2, Task3 as separate columns gstudy(mvbind(Task1, Task2, Task3) ~ (1|Person) + (1|Rater), data = data_wide) # Long format: single Score column with Subtest indicator gstudy(bf(Score ~ 0 + Subtest + (0+Subtest|r|Person), sigma ~ 0 + Subtest),         data = data_long, backend = "brms")

title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: G-theory variance components can estimated using several methods, distinct advantages limitations. facet package supports three estimation approaches: Method Moments (ANOVA-based), Restricted Maximum Likelihood (via lme4), Bayesian estimation (via brms/Stan).

previous_headings: Estimation Methods
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Understanding variance component estimation requires understanding Expected Mean Squares (EMS). ANOVA source, EMS expresses mean square estimates terms population variance components. Method Moments, REML, estimation approaches build framework.

previous_headings: Estimation Methods > Expected Mean Squares
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Consider balanced design npn_p persons respond nin_i items. ANOVA table : Derivation EMS Person Effect: model Xpi=μ+μp+μi+μpi+epiX_{pi} = \mu + \mu_p + \mu_i + \mu_{pi} + e_{pi}. person sum squares : SSp=ni∑p=1np(X‾p−X‾)2SS_p = n_i \sum_{p=1}^{n_p} (\bar{X}_p - \bar{X})^2 X‾p=1ni∑iXpi\bar{X}_p = \frac{1}{n_i}\sum_i X_{pi} X‾\bar{X} grand mean. expected value SSpSS_p requires computing E(X‾p−X‾)2E(\bar{X}_p - \bar{X})^2. First, express X‾p\bar{X}_p terms model: X‾p=μ+μp+μ‾+μ‾pi+e‾p\bar{X}_p = \mu + \mu_p + \bar{\mu}_i + \bar{\mu}_{pi} + \bar{e}_p bars indicate averaging items. random effects, E(μp2)=σp2E(\mu_p^2) = \sigma^2_p, E(μi2)=σi2E(\mu_i^2) = \sigma^2_i, E(μpi2)=σpi2E(\mu_{pi}^2) = \sigma^2_{pi}, E(epi2)=σe2E(e_{pi}^2) = \sigma^2_e. algebraic manipulation: E(MSp)=E(SSpnp−1)=σe2+niσpi2+niσp2E(MS_p) = E\left(\frac{SS_p}{n_p-1}\right) = \sigma^2_e + n_i\sigma^2_{pi} + n_i\sigma^2_p key insight person mean square contains variance three sources: residual error, person ×\times item interaction (multiplied nin_i person mean averages nin_i items), person variance (also multiplied nin_i). EMS three terms — intuition. compute variance person’s mean across nin_i items, random part score gets averaged . Specifically: residual error (σe2\sigma^2_e) shrinks factor nin_i person sees nin_i items errors partially cancel. person ×\times item interaction also shrinks factor nin_i, interaction random component varies item item therefore averages across items. person effect, however, constant across items — shrink mean; multiplied nin_i mean square (mean square sum squares divided df, sum squares constant effect across nin_i items scales nin_i). E(MSp)E(MS_p) σe2+niσpi2+niσp2\sigma^2_e + n_i \sigma^2_{pi} + n_i \sigma^2_p: piece equation different combination “shrinks averaging” “stays constant across items.”

previous_headings: Estimation Methods > Expected Mean Squares
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Given EMS, solve variance components algebraically: Start bottom ANOVA table (highest-order interaction) Subtract appropriate mean squares isolate components p×ip \times design: σ̂pi2=MSpi−MSe1\hat{\sigma}^2_{pi} = \frac{MS_{pi} - MS_e}{1} practice, MSpiMS_{pi} lowest-order term, : σ̂pi2=MSpi−MSresidual\hat{\sigma}^2_{pi} = MS_{pi} - MS_{residual} balanced designs, estimate: σ̂pi2=MSpi−MSresidual\hat{\sigma}^2_{pi} = MS_{pi} - MS_{residual} person variance: σ̂p2=MSp−MSpini\hat{\sigma}^2_p = \frac{MS_p - MS_{pi}}{n_i} item variance: σ̂i2=MSi−MSpinp\hat{\sigma}^2_i = \frac{MS_i - MS_{pi}}{n_p}

what: EMS for the Nested brennan Design
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: brennan data Person ×\times Task ×\times (Rater:Task) design — Person crossed Task, Rater nested within Task, nrn_r raters per task. model Xptr=μ+τp+αt+ρr(t)+(τα)pt+eptrX_{ptr} = \mu + \tau_p + \alpha_t + \rho_{r(t)} + (\tau\alpha)_{pt} + e_{ptr} eptre_{ptr} absorbs Person ×\times Rater:Task interaction (cell single observation, effect confounded residual). EMS table balanced nested design npn_p persons, ntn_t tasks, nrn_r raters per task : Solving EMS equations (Henderson’s Method ) nested design, bottom-order: σ̂e2=MSe,σ̂pt2=MSpt−MSenr,σ̂r:t2=MSr:t−MSe−σ̂pt2np\hat{\sigma}^2_e = MS_e, \qquad \hat{\sigma}^2_{pt} = \frac{MS_{pt} - MS_e}{n_r}, \qquad \hat{\sigma}^2_{r:t} = \frac{MS_{r:t} - MS_e - \hat{\sigma}^2_{pt}}{n_p} σ̂p2=MSp−nrσ̂pt2−MSentnr,σ̂t2=MSt−npσ̂r:t2−nrσ̂pt2−MSenpnr\hat{\sigma}^2_p = \frac{MS_p - n_r \hat{\sigma}^2_{pt} - MS_e}{n_t n_r}, \qquad \hat{\sigma}^2_t = \frac{MS_t - n_p \hat{\sigma}^2_{r:t} - n_r \hat{\sigma}^2_{pt} - MS_e}{n_p n_r}

previous_headings: Estimation Methods > Method of Moments (MOM)
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: method moments approach variance component estimation deep roots statistical methodology. Henderson (1953) developed three methods (Methods , II, III) variance component estimation ANOVA, providing foundational approach balanced unbalanced designs. Henderson’s Method standard ANOVA approach used balanced G-theory designs. Searle, Casella, & McCulloch (1992) provided definitive modern treatment Variance Components, including comprehensive coverage ANOVA methods, problem negative estimates, relationship likelihood-based approaches. Hartley & Rao (1967) established connection ANOVA maximum likelihood methods, bridging classical modern approaches variance component estimation. Satterthwaite (1941, 1946) developed approximation confidence intervals variance components, treating linear combinations mean squares scaled chi-square variates. approximation remains standard MOM confidence intervals implemented facet package. Brennan (2001) synthesized methods specifically G-theory, providing rules constructing Expected Mean Squares (EMS) implemented MOM backend.

previous_headings: Estimation Methods > Method of Moments (MOM)
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Method Moments, also known ANOVA-based estimation, derives variance components Expected Mean Squares (EMS). balanced two-facet crossed design (p×ip \times ), ANOVA table yields: Solving system EMS equations: σ̂pi2=MSpi−MSen\hat{\sigma}^2_{pi} = \frac{MS_{pi} - MS_e}{n} σ̂p2=MSp−MSpini\hat{\sigma}^2_p = \frac{MS_p - MS_{pi}}{n_i} σ̂i2=MSi−MSpinp\hat{\sigma}^2_i = \frac{MS_i - MS_{pi}}{n_p} unbalanced designs, EMS equations become complex. facet package detects crossing patterns data computes appropriate effective sample sizes.

previous_headings: Estimation Methods > Method of Moments (MOM)
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Henderson’s Method (Henderson 1953) standard ANOVA approach used balanced G-theory designs. procedure : Compute ANOVA table Derive EMS source Solve system EMS equations algebraically Example: p×ip \times design 10 persons 5 items MSp=15.2MS_p = 15.2, MSi=8.7MS_i = 8.7, MSpi=3.4MS_{pi} = 3.4: σ̂p2=15.2−3.45=11.85=2.36\hat{\sigma}^2_p = \frac{15.2 - 3.4}{5} = \frac{11.8}{5} = 2.36 σ̂i2=8.7−3.410=5.310=0.53\hat{\sigma}^2_i = \frac{8.7 - 3.4}{10} = \frac{5.3}{10} = 0.53 σ̂pi2=3.4\hat{\sigma}^2_{pi} = 3.4 interpretation: universe score variance (person) 2.36, item difficulty variance 0.53, person ×\times item interaction 3.4. Total variance = 6.29, person variance accounts 37.5% total variance.

previous_headings: Estimation Methods > Method of Moments (MOM)
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: (Satterthwaite 1941) (Satterthwaite 1946) developed approximate distribution linear combinations mean squares. variance component σ̂2=(MSa−MSb)/n\hat{\sigma}^2 = (MS_a - MS_b)/n: approximate degrees freedom : dfapprox=(MSa−MSb)2MSa2dfa+MSb2dfbdf_{approx} = \frac{(MS_a - MS_b)^2}{\frac{MS_a^2}{df_a} + \frac{MS_b^2}{df_b}} standard error : SE(σ̂2)≈2⋅MS2dfSE(\hat{\sigma}^2) \approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot MS^2}{df}} Confidence intervals use chi-square distribution: CI=df⋅σ̂2χ1−α/2,df2 df⋅σ̂2χα/2,df2CI = \frac{df \cdot \hat{\sigma}^2}{\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2, df}} \text{ } \frac{df \cdot \hat{\sigma}^2}{\chi^2_{\alpha/2, df}} chi-square-based CI asymmetric respects boundary constraint (variance ≥\geq 0). simpler Wald-type approximation σ̂2±zα/2⋅SE(σ̂2)\hat{\sigma}^2 \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot SE(\hat{\sigma}^2) also used may produce negative lower bounds truncated zero. work reasonably large degrees freedom may inaccurate small samples. worked Satterthwaite example. Suppose p×ip \times design np=10n_p = 10 persons ni=5n_i = 5 items, MSp=15.2MS_p = 15.2, MSpi=3.4MS_{pi} = 3.4, dfp=9df_p = 9, dfpi=36df_{pi} = 36. person variance estimate σ̂p2=(15.2−3.4)/5=2.36\hat{\sigma}^2_p = (15.2 - 3.4) / 5 = 2.36. approximate degrees freedom : dfapprox=(15.2−3.4)2(15.22/9)+(3.42/36)=139.125.7+0.32≈5.4df_{approx} = \frac{(15.2 - 3.4)^2}{(15.2^2 / 9) + (3.4^2 / 36)} = \frac{139.1}{25.7 + 0.32} \approx 5.4 95% CI uses χ0.975,5.42\chi^2_{0.975, 5.4} χ0.025,5.42\chi^2_{0.025, 5.4}: CI=[5.4×2.3612.83,5.4×2.360.51]≈[0.99,24.98]CI = \left[ \frac{5.4 \times 2.36}{12.83}, \frac{5.4 \times 2.36}{0.51} \right] \approx [0.99, 24.98] Note wide CI — [1.0, 25.0] — even though point estimate 2.36. wide CI reflects two things: (1) small sample size, (2) fact estimating difference two mean squares, effective df much smaller df either MS alone. np=50n_p = 50 persons ni=20n_i = 20 items, CI shrink roughly [1.6, 3.7]. practical lesson: Satterthwaite df often binding constraint CI width, raw sample size.

previous_headings: Estimation Methods > Restricted Maximum Likelihood (REML)
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Patterson & Thompson (1971) introduced Restricted Maximum Likelihood, recognizing ML estimators variance components biased downward account degrees freedom lost estimating fixed effects. approach partitions likelihood independent components: one containing information variance components another fixed effects. Harville (1974, 1977) established theoretical foundation showing REML estimators reduce bias compared ML, bias reduction particularly important small samples. Harville (1977) demonstrated ML estimators variance components negatively biased, REML estimators reduce eliminate bias. Searle, Casella, & McCulloch (1992) provided comprehensive treatment REML estimation, including computational algorithms relationship ANOVA methods (balanced designs, REML estimates equal ANOVA estimates). Bates et al. (2015) developed lme4 package implementing efficient REML estimation using sparse matrix methods Cholesky decomposition, enabling analysis complex crossed designs previously computationally infeasible. lme4 backend facet leverages advances, providing REML estimation profile likelihood confidence intervals variance components.

previous_headings: Estimation Methods > Restricted Maximum Likelihood (REML)
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Restricted Maximum Likelihood (REML) estimates variance components maximizing likelihood linear combinations data eliminate fixed effects. linear mixed model: 𝐲=𝐗𝛃+𝐙𝐮+𝛜\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \mathbf{Z}\mathbf{u} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon} : 𝐲\mathbf{y} response vector 𝐗\mathbf{X} fixed-effects design matrix 𝛃\boldsymbol{\beta} fixed-effects vector 𝐙\mathbf{Z} random-effects design matrix 𝐮∼N(𝟎,𝐆)\mathbf{u} \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{G}) random effects 𝛜∼N(𝟎,𝐑)\boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \mathbf{R}) residual error variance-covariance matrix 𝐲\mathbf{y} : 𝐕=𝐙𝐆𝐙′+𝐑\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{Z}\mathbf{G}\mathbf{Z}' + \mathbf{R} REML log-likelihood : ℓREML(𝛉)=−12[log|𝐕|+𝐲′𝐏𝐲+log|𝐗′𝐕−1𝐗|]\ell_{REML}(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\frac{1}{2}\left[\log|\mathbf{V}| + \mathbf{y}'\mathbf{P}\mathbf{y} + \log|\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{X}|\right] 𝐏=𝐕−1−𝐕−1𝐗(𝐗′𝐕−1𝐗)−1𝐗′𝐕−1\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{V}^{-1} - \mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{V}^{-1}\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{V}^{-1} projection matrix removes fixed effects. REML preferred ML accounts degrees freedom lost estimating fixed effects, producing less biased variance component estimates.

text: growing body research advocated Bayesian estimation G-theory models, providing full posterior distributions variance components G-coefficients rather point estimates. Putka & Hoffman (2013) pioneered application Bayesian G-theory assessment center data, demonstrating advantages ANOVA-based approaches. LoPilato, Carter, & Wang (2015) provided foundational simulation evidence prior selection posterior summarization management research. Jackson colleagues applied properly specified Bayesian G-theory models assessment center performance rating data, addressing specific problem applied literature: prior AC research often used mis-specified G-theory models omitted interaction terms, leading confounded variance component estimates. properly specified G-theory model, relevant interaction terms included design (Cronbach et al. (1972); Brennan (2001)). Jackson et al.’s contributions twofold: Substantive findings: Using Bayesian G-theory fully crossed Person ×\times Exercise ×\times Rater designs, showed dimension-related variance negligibly small AC ratings (Jackson et al. (2016)), finding replicated multisource performance ratings (Jackson et al. (2020)). Subsequent work showed reliability ACs depends general exercise effects, dimensions (Jackson, Michaelides, Dewberry, Nelson, et al. (2022)), uncertainty whether rater variance constitutes signal error fundamentally impacts reliability estimates supervisor ratings (Jackson, Michaelides, Dewberry, Jones, et al. (2022)). Interpretive reframing: Jackson et al. (2023) demonstrated G-theory random effects models CFA models yield equivalent variance components, generalizability coefficients, latent scores, arguing G-theory can address reliability structural validity questions—just reliability traditionally assumed. Jiang & Skorupski (2018) developed Bayesian estimation methods multivariate G-theory, handling variance-covariance matrices providing uncertainty quantification multivariate designs BUGS/JAGS implementations.

previous_headings: Estimation Methods > Bayesian Estimation with NUTS/HMC
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Bayesian estimation treats variance components random variables prior distributions. posterior distribution : p(𝛉|𝐲)∝p(𝐲|𝛉)⋅p(𝛉)p(\boldsymbol{\theta} | \mathbf{y}) \propto p(\mathbf{y} | \boldsymbol{\theta}) \cdot p(\boldsymbol{\theta}) p(𝛉)p(\boldsymbol{\theta}) prior p(𝐲|𝛉)p(\mathbf{y} | \boldsymbol{\theta}) likelihood. Prior distributions variance components must positive-valued. Common choices: Half-Cauchy: p(σ)∝11+σ2/s2p(\sigma) \propto \frac{1}{1 + \sigma^2/s^2} (recommended variance parameters) Exponential: p(σ)=λe−λσp(\sigma) = \lambda e^{-\lambda\sigma} (simple, one-parameter) Inverse-Gamma: p(σ2)∝(σ2)−(α+1)e−β/σ2p(\sigma^2) \propto (\sigma^2)^{-(\alpha+1)} e^{-\beta/\sigma^2} (traditional problematic) half-Cauchy prior recommended heavy tails (allowing large variance values) concentrating probability mass near zero small effects. Hamiltonian Monte Carlo (HMC) gradient-based MCMC algorithm exploits geometry posterior distribution. treats negative log-posterior potential energy surface simulates Hamiltonian dynamics: H(𝛉,𝐩)=U(𝛉)+K(𝐩)H(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{p}) = U(\boldsymbol{\theta}) + K(\mathbf{p}) U(𝛉)=−log⁡p(𝛉|𝐲)U(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = -\log p(\boldsymbol{\theta} | \mathbf{y}) potential energy K(𝐩)=12𝐩′𝐌−1𝐩K(\mathbf{p}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{p}'\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathbf{p} kinetic energy. -U-Turn Sampler (NUTS) automatically tunes HMC parameters: Step size ϵ\epsilon (adapted warmup) Number leapfrog steps LL (determined dynamically) Mass matrix 𝐌\mathbf{M} (adapted posterior geometry) HMC intuition. Imagine posterior distribution landscape varying elevation, height = negative log-posterior density. Standard MCMC (e.g., Metropolis-Hastings) takes small random steps can get stuck narrow valleys wander aimlessly across high-density plateaus. HMC treats chain frictionless particle rolling landscape randomly assigned momentum. particle allowed roll number leapfrog steps, position recorded new sample. leapfrog integrator approximately conserves Hamiltonian dynamics, sampler moves long, informed trajectories efficiently explore posterior’s geometry. NUTS extension automates choice trajectory length: stops particle starts “double back” (“U-turn”), user specify number leapfrog steps. practical payoff G-theory: HMC handles ratios variance components (G, Phi) gracefully, full posterior sampled — need delta-method bootstrap approximations frequentist CIs require derived quantities.

previous_headings: Estimation Methods > Bayesian Estimation with NUTS/HMC
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: R-hat (R̂\hat{R}) compares within-chain -chain variance: R̂=var̂(ψ)W\hat{R} = \sqrt{\frac{\widehat{var}(\psi)}{W}} var̂(ψ)\widehat{var}(\psi) combines within-chain (WW) -chain (BB) variance. Values near 1.0 indicate convergence; values > 1.01 suggest non-convergence. Effective Sample Size (ESS) estimates number independent samples: ESS=N1+2∑k=1∞ρkESS = \frac{N}{1 + 2\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\rho_k} ρk\rho_k autocorrelation lag kk. Higher ESS indicates better mixing. Target: ESS>400ESS > 400 reliable inference.

previous_headings: Estimation Methods > Bayesian Estimation with NUTS/HMC
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Bayesian inference provides: Posterior mean: E[σ2|𝐲]E[\sigma^2 | \mathbf{y}] (point estimate) Posterior SD: SD[σ2|𝐲]SD[\sigma^2 | \mathbf{y}] (uncertainty) Credible intervals: [σ0.0252,σ0.9752][\sigma^2_{0.025}, \sigma^2_{0.975}] (95% CI) Full posterior distribution: Samples p(σ2|𝐲)p(\sigma^2 | \mathbf{y}) D-study coefficients, posterior distribution propagates variance component uncertainty: p(g|𝐲)=∫p(g|𝛔2)⋅p(𝛔2|𝐲)d𝛔2p(g | \mathbf{y}) = \int p(g | \boldsymbol{\sigma}^2) \cdot p(\boldsymbol{\sigma}^2 | \mathbf{y}) \, d\boldsymbol{\sigma}^2 naturally incorporates uncertainty coefficient estimates.

title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: backend = "brms" option uses brms::brm() compiles models Stan: implementation: Converts formula brms syntax Compiles model Stan Runs NUTS sampling (default: 4 chains, 2000 iterations) Extracts posterior draws variance components Computes posterior summaries diagnostics
code: # Bayesian G-study with default priors g_brms <- gstudy(Score ~ (1|Person) + (1|Task),                   data = brennan, backend = "brms")  # With custom priors library(brms) my_prior <- c(   set_prior("exponential(1)", class = "sd", group = "Person"),   set_prior("exponential(0.5)", class = "sd", group = "Task"),   set_prior("exponential(1)", class = "sigma") ) g_custom <- gstudy(Score ~ (1|Person) + (1|Task),                     data = brennan, backend = "brms", prior = my_prior)  # Multivariate Bayesian G-study (requires a fully crossed design; # see the `crossed_wide` data defined in the Multivariate G-Study section) g_mv <- gstudy(mvbind(Task1, Task2, Task3) ~ (1|Person) + (1|Rater),                 data = crossed_wide, backend = "brms")

text: variance components (standard deviations), brms uses weakly informative priors default: σ∼Student-t3(0,σdata)\sigma \sim \text{Student-}t_3(0, \sigma_{\text{data}}) prior: bounded zero (appropriate standard deviations) heavy tails (allows large values supported data) weakly informative (centered scale data)

previous_headings: Estimation Methods > Bayesian Estimation with NUTS/HMC
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Half-Cauchy Prior (Gelman 2006): σ∼Half-Cauchy(0,s)\sigma \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0, s) density: p(σ)=2πs(1+(σ/s)2),σ>0p(\sigma) = \frac{2}{\pi s \left(1 + (\sigma/s)^2\right)}, \quad \sigma > 0 Properties: - Heavy tails allow large variance values - Concentrates mass near zero small effects - Scale parameter ss controls spread Exponential Prior: simplicity, exponential prior provides one-parameter alternative: σ∼Exponential(λ)\sigma \sim \text{Exponential}(\lambda) rate parameter λ\lambda: p(σ)=λe−λσ,σ>0p(\sigma) = \lambda e^{-\lambda\sigma}, \quad \sigma > 0 expected value E[σ]=1/λE[\sigma] = 1/\lambda. Setting λ=1\lambda = 1 gives prior centered σ=1\sigma = 1. half-Cauchy preferred inverse-gamma. traditional prior variance parameters (.e., σ2\sigma^2, σ\sigma) inverse-gamma family. serious problem: inverse-gamma pole zero pulls posterior away zero, even data want . well-documented pathology mixed models — true variance component small, inverse-gamma prior can keep posterior artificially away zero, leading overestimation. half-Cauchy, contrast, cusp zero: concentrates mass near zero (good small effects) heavy tails allow large values supported data. See Gelman (2006) full argument. practical rule: always prefer half-Cauchy Student-t family variance parameters; avoid inverse-gamma unless strong prior information variance bounded away zero. Regularizing Priors (code examples):
code: # Weakly informative (recommended for most applications) prior_weak <- c(   set_prior("cauchy(0, 2)", class = "sd"),   set_prior("cauchy(0, 2)", class = "sigma") )  # Slightly informative (when some variance components expected small) prior_reg <- c(   set_prior("exponential(1)", class = "sd"),   set_prior("exponential(1)", class = "sigma") )  # Strongly regularizing (for very small samples or complex designs) prior_strong <- c(   set_prior("exponential(2)", class = "sd"),   set_prior("exponential(2)", class = "sigma") )

title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: grand mean (intercept), default priors typically uninformative: μ∼𝒩(0,10)\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 10) informative scale measurement known: HMC matters specifically G-theory. G-theory produces derived quantities — G Phi coefficients — nonlinear functions (ratios) variance components. frequentist estimation, way get CI ratio delta method (linear approximation can inaccurate small samples) bootstrap (computationally expensive noisy resamples). HMC’s full posterior samples let compute ratio every posterior draw, giving exact credible intervals G Phi asymptotic approximations. credible intervals see dstudy(..., ci = c("g", "phi")) computed way: draw variance components produces draw G, 2.5th/97.5th percentiles G draws CI limits. one main reasons use Bayesian estimation need defensible uncertainty quantification reliability coefficients.

previous_headings: Using the facet Package
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: facet package includes two classic G-theory datasets: brennan dataset contains scores 10 persons across 3 tasks, panel 4 raters nested within task (12 raters total). design p×t×(r:t)p \times t \times (r:t): Person crossed Task, Rater nested within Task (raters 1–4 see Task 1, raters 5–8 see Task 2, raters 9–12 see Task 3). ideal demonstrating nested designs. Design note (brennan). Rater nested within Task, random-effects formula must use (1 | Rater:Task), (1 | Rater). model decomposes observed score four estimable random-effect variances — σPerson2\sigma^2_{\text{Person}} (universe-score variance), σTask2\sigma^2_{\text{Task}} (task-difficulty variance), σRater:Task2\sigma^2_{\text{Rater:Task}} (within-task rater-leniency variance), σPerson:Task2\sigma^2_{\text{Person:Task}} (person--task interaction) — plus residual absorbs person--rater:task interaction (Person ×\times Rater:Task cell single observation, effect confounded residual). crossed formula Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater) treat Rater crossed Task, supported data. rajaratnam dataset contains scores 8 persons across 3 subtests, items nested within subtest. Items labeled within subtest (e.g., “Item 1” Subtest 1 different item “Item 1” Subtest 2), random-effects formula uses Item:Subtest capture unique item-within-subtest factor. ideal demonstrating nested designs.
code: library(facet) library(broom) library(dplyr)  # Brennan (2001) dataset: Person crossed with Task and Rater data(brennan) head(brennan) #>   Task Person Rater Score y #> 1    1      1     1     5 5 #> 2    1      2     1     9 9 #> 3    1      3     1     3 3 #> 4    1      4     1     7 7 #> 5    1      5     1     9 9 #> 6    1      6     1     3 3  # Rajaratnam, Cronbach, & Gleser (1965): Person crossed with Subtest,  # Items nested within Subtest data(rajaratnam) head(rajaratnam) #>   Person Subtest Item Score #> 1      1       1    1     4 #> 2      2       1    1     2 #> 3      3       1    1     2 #> 4      4       1    1     1 #> 5      5       1    1     3 #> 6      6       1    1     1 Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) + (1 | Person:Task)

previous_headings: Using the facet Package
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: G-study estimates variance components facet measurement design. formula syntax follows lme4 conventions, specifying random effects (1 | facet). output shows estimated variance components: Person: Variance attributable differences persons (universe score variance) Task: Variance attributable differences task difficulty Residual: Remaining variance (person ×\times task interaction + error)
code: # Simple G-study with default REML backend g <- gstudy(Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task),             data = brennan)  # Print basic results print(g) #> Generalizability Study (G-Study) #> ================================ #>  #> Backend: lme4  #> Formula: Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task)  #> Number of observations: 120  #> Multivariate: No  #>  #> Object of measurement: Person  #> Facets: Person, Task  #>  #> Sample Sizes: #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   effect            type         n #>   <chr>             <chr>    <int> #> 1 Person            main        10 #> 2 Task              main         3 #> 3 Person:Task (res) residual    30 #>  #> Variance Components: #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   component estimate   pct #>   <chr>        <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 Person       0.584  13.3 #> 2 Task         0.52   11.8 #> 3 Residual     3.29   74.9

text: Use summary() detailed view options display variance SD scale: Extract variance components tibble using tidy(): Use glance() one-row summary G-study:
code: # Summary with variance estimates (default) summary(g) #> === G-Study Summary === #>  #> Design Information: #>  Backend: lme4  #>  Formula: Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task)  #>  Observations: 120  #>  Multivariate: No  #>  #> Facet Information: #>  Object of measurement: Person  #>  Facets: Person, Task  #>  #> Sample Sizes: #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   effect            type         n #>   <chr>             <chr>    <int> #> 1 Person            main        10 #> 2 Task              main         3 #> 3 Person:Task (res) residual    30 #>  #> Variance Components: #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   component estimate   pct #>   <chr>        <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 Person       0.584  13.3 #> 2 Task         0.52   11.8 #> 3 Residual     3.29   74.9 #>  #> Total variance: 4.397  # Summary with standard deviation estimates summary(g, scale = "sd") #> === G-Study Summary === #>  #> Design Information: #>  Backend: lme4  #>  Formula: Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task)  #>  Observations: 120  #>  Multivariate: No  #>  #> Facet Information: #>  Object of measurement: Person  #>  Facets: Person, Task  #>  #> Sample Sizes: #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   effect            type         n #>   <chr>             <chr>    <int> #> 1 Person            main        10 #> 2 Task              main         3 #> 3 Person:Task (res) residual    30 #>  #> Variance Components: #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   component estimate   pct #>   <chr>        <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 Person       0.764  13.3 #> 2 Task         0.721  11.8 #> 3 Residual     1.82   74.9 #>  #> Total variance: 4.397 tidy(g) #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   component dim   type       var   pct #>   <chr>     <chr> <chr>    <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 Person    Score main     0.584  13.3 #> 2 Task      Score main     0.52   11.8 #> 3 Residual  Score residual 3.29   74.9 glance(g) #> # A tibble: 1 × 5 #>   n_obs n_facets backend is_multivariate object #>   <int>    <int> <chr>   <lgl>           <chr>  #> 1   120        2 lme4    FALSE           Person

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Basic G-Study
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: pct column shows percentage total variance component accounts . helps identify facets contribute measurement error: Interpreting variance component percentages. pct column tells measurement error coming . rough rule thumb: Person component less 30% total variance, measurement dominated facets individual differences — meaning instrument “noisy” sense hard distinguish persons one another. single facet (Task, Rater, etc.) accounts 50% variance, facet clear target design improvement: adding levels facet buy largest gain reliability. cumulative percentage column useful identifying top 2-3 sources error — facets worth investing next measurement campaign.
code: # Components ordered by variance tidy(g) %>%   arrange(desc(var)) %>%   mutate(pct_cumulative = cumsum(pct)) #> # A tibble: 3 × 6 #>   component dim   type       var   pct pct_cumulative #>   <chr>     <chr> <chr>    <dbl> <dbl>          <dbl> #> 1 Residual  Score residual 3.29   74.9           74.9 #> 2 Person    Score main     0.584  13.3           88.2 #> 3 Task      Score main     0.52   11.8          100

title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: lme4 backend, can request confidence intervals using ci_method parameter.

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > G-Study with Confidence Intervals
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Profile likelihood CIs accurate Wald-type intervals slower compute. Note: Brennan dataset, Raters nested within Task (Task set 4 raters), include crossed Rater effects Task:
code: # G-study with profile likelihood confidence intervals # Note: Rater is nested within Task, so we use Rater:Task notation g_profile <- gstudy(   Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) +     (1 | Person:Task),   data = brennan,   ci_method = "profile" )  summary(g_profile) #> === G-Study Summary === #>  #> Design Information: #>  Backend: lme4  #>  Formula: Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) + (1 | Person:Task)  #>  Observations: 120  #>  Multivariate: No  #>  #> Facet Information: #>  Object of measurement: Person  #>  Facets: Person, Task, Rater  #>  #> Sample Sizes: #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   effect             type            n #>   <chr>              <chr>       <dbl> #> 1 Person             main           10 #> 2 Task               main            3 #> 3 Rater:Task         interaction    12 #> 4 Person:Task        interaction    30 #> 5 Person:Rater (res) residual      120 #> 6 Rater per Task     nested          4 #>  #> Nested details: #>   Rater per Task: 3 Tasks, 4.0 Raters per Task #>  #> Variance Components: #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   component   estimate   pct `2.5% CI` `97.5% CI` #>   <chr>          <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 Person         0.473 10.8      0           1.94 #> 2 Task           0.325  7.42     0           3.56 #> 3 Rater:Task     0.647 14.8      0.149       2.28 #> 4 Person:Task    0.56  12.8      0           1.88 #> 5 Residual       2.38  54.3      1.78        3.29 #>  #> Total variance: 4.385

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > G-Study with Confidence Intervals
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Parametric bootstrap CIs accurate slowest: production use, increase nsim 500-1000 stable CI estimates.
code: # G-study with bootstrap confidence intervals (50 iterations for speed) g_boot <- gstudy(   Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task),   data = brennan,   ci_method = "boot",   nsim = 50,   boot.type = "perc" ) #> ================================================================================  summary(g_boot) #> === G-Study Summary === #>  #> Design Information: #>  Backend: lme4  #>  Formula: Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task)  #>  Observations: 120  #>  Multivariate: No  #>  #> Facet Information: #>  Object of measurement: Person  #>  Facets: Person, Task  #>  #> Sample Sizes: #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   effect            type         n #>   <chr>             <chr>    <int> #> 1 Person            main        10 #> 2 Task              main         3 #> 3 Person:Task (res) residual    30 #>  #> Variance Components: #> # A tibble: 3 × 5 #>   component estimate   pct `2.5% CI` `97.5% CI` #>   <chr>        <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 Person       0.584  13.3     0.004       1.22 #> 2 Task         0.52   11.8     0           1.92 #> 3 Residual     3.29   74.9     2.29        4.32 #>  #> Total variance: 4.397

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > G-Study with Confidence Intervals
what: Profile Likelihood vs Bootstrap: A Side-by-Side Comparison
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: two CI methods answer question (true variance component?) different tradeoffs. following chunk fits model methods prints CIs together: Reading comparison. small balanced design, two methods usually agree closely point estimates can differ CI width — particularly components near zero, profile-likelihood CI may underestimate uncertainty. bootstrap honest boundary effects also noisier nsim small (50; publication use 500+). practical rule: use ci_method = "profile" routine reporting balanced near-balanced designs, ci_method = "boot" components near zero, want defend “variance plausibly zero” claim, reviewers want conservative uncertainty quantification.
code: # Fit the same model with both methods g_profile <- gstudy(   Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) +     (1 | Person:Task),   data = brennan,   ci_method = "profile" )  # Compare the CIs profile_ci <- tidy(g_profile) %>%   select(component, var, lower, upper) %>%   mutate(method = "profile")  # Bootstrap CIs were computed above in g_boot boot_ci <- tidy(g_boot) %>%   select(component, var, lower, upper) %>%   mutate(method = "boot (nsim=50)")  bind_rows(profile_ci, boot_ci) %>%   select(method, component, var, lower, upper) %>%   mutate(across(c(var, lower, upper), ~ sprintf("%.3f", .x))) #> # A tibble: 8 × 5 #>   method         component   var   lower upper #>   <chr>          <chr>       <chr> <chr> <chr> #> 1 profile        Person      0.473 0.000 1.945 #> 2 profile        Task        0.325 0.000 3.562 #> 3 profile        Rater:Task  0.647 0.149 2.282 #> 4 profile        Person:Task 0.560 0.000 1.883 #> 5 profile        Residual    2.380 1.776 3.293 #> 6 boot (nsim=50) Person      0.584 0.004 1.221 #> 7 boot (nsim=50) Task        0.520 0.000 1.924 #> 8 boot (nsim=50) Residual    3.293 2.294 4.319

title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Method Moments backend uses ANOVA-based variance component estimation: MOM automatically provides asymptotic confidence intervals. approach works best balanced designs.
code: # Method of moments G-study with nested design (Rater nested in Task) g_mom <- gstudy(   Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) +     (1 | Person:Task),   data = brennan,   backend = "mom" )  summary(g_mom) #> === G-Study Summary === #>  #> Design Information: #>  Backend: mom  #>  Formula: Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) + (1 | Person:Task)  #>  Observations: 120  #>  Multivariate: No  #>  #> Facet Information: #>  Object of measurement: Person  #>  Facets: Person, Task, Rater  #>  #> Sample Sizes: #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   effect             type            n #>   <chr>              <chr>       <dbl> #> 1 Person             main           10 #> 2 Task               main            3 #> 3 Rater:Task         interaction    12 #> 4 Person:Task        interaction    30 #> 5 Person:Rater (res) residual      120 #> 6 Rater per Task     nested          4 #>  #> Nested details: #>   Rater per Task: 3 Tasks, 4.0 Raters per Task #>  #> Variance Components: #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   component   estimate   pct `2.5% CI` `97.5% CI` #>   <chr>          <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 Person         0.473 10.7       0          1.30 #> 2 Task           0.381  8.58      0          1.44 #> 3 Rater:Task     0.648 14.6       0          1.47 #> 4 Person:Task    0.56  12.6       0          1.34 #> 5 Residual       2.38  53.6       1.65       3.11 #>  #> Total variance: 4.442

title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: nested designs, specify nesting structure using : notation:
code: # Items nested within subtests (rajaratnam dataset) g_nested <- gstudy(   Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Subtest) + (1 | Item:Subtest) +     (1 | Person:Subtest),   data = rajaratnam,   backend = "mom" )  summary(g_nested) #> === G-Study Summary === #>  #> Design Information: #>  Backend: mom  #>  Formula: Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Subtest) + (1 | Item:Subtest) + (1 |      Person:Subtest)  #>  Observations: 64  #>  Multivariate: No  #>  #> Facet Information: #>  Object of measurement: Person  #>  Facets: Person, Subtest, Item  #>  #> Sample Sizes: #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>   effect                    type            n #>   <chr>                     <chr>       <int> #> 1 Person                    main            8 #> 2 Subtest                   main            3 #> 3 Item:Subtest              interaction     8 #> 4 Person:Subtest            interaction    24 #> 5 Person:Subtest:Item (res) residual       64 #>  #> Variance Components: #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   component      estimate   pct `2.5% CI` `97.5% CI` #>   <chr>             <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 Person            1.26  20.4      0          3.05  #> 2 Subtest           2.85  46.1      0          8.73  #> 3 Item:Subtest      0.295  4.77     0          0.793 #> 4 Person:Subtest    0.929 15.0      0          1.86  #> 5 Residual          0.843 13.6      0.448      1.24  #>  #> Total variance: 6.18

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Bayesian G-Study
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: simplest Bayesian G-study uses default priors. Rater nested within Task, formula uses (1 | Rater:Task) (1 | Person:Task) (see callout “Built-Datasets” section):
code: # Bayesian G-study with default priors g_bayes <- gstudy(   Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) +     (1 | Person:Task),   data = brennan,   backend = "brms" )  print(g_bayes) summary(g_bayes)

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Bayesian G-Study
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: output includes: Convergence diagnostics interpretation: R-hat (R̂\hat{R}) compares within-chain -chain variance: R̂=var̂(ψ)W\hat{R} = \sqrt{\frac{\widehat{\text{var}}(\psi)}{W}} var̂(ψ)=n−1nW+1nB\widehat{\text{var}}(\psi) = \frac{n-1}{n}W + \frac{1}{n}B WW = within-chain variance BB = -chain variance. Values < 1.01 indicate convergence Values > 1.05 suggest problems Effective Sample Size (ESS) estimates number independent samples: ESS=N1+2∑k=1∞ρk\text{ESS} = \frac{N}{1 + 2\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\rho_k} Target: ESS > 400 reliable inference Higher ESS indicates better mixing Reading R-hat ESS practice. Compare values across components, just fixed thresholds. common pattern: R-hat ESS fine Person main facet components, one component (often small near-zero interaction) shows R-hat = 1.04 Bulk_ESS = 300. usually means component poorly identified data — isn’t enough information pin value precisely, sampler drifting. variance estimate may still reasonable, credible interval component treated approximate. ESS targets: >1000 comfortable, 400-1000 acceptable, <400 means credible intervals may unreliable trust tail-based inferences (e.g., “probability variance > X”).

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Bayesian G-Study
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Control MCMC sampling additional arguments: Guidelines: Use 4 chains reliable convergence assessment Use 2000+ iterations complex models Warmup 1000 typically sufficient Thinning rarely needed HMC
code: g_full <- gstudy(   Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) +     (1 | Person:Task),   data = brennan,   backend = "brms",   chains = 4,        # Number of chains   iter = 4000,       # Total iterations per chain   warmup = 1000,     # Warmup iterations   thin = 1,          # Thinning interval   cores = 4,         # Parallel processing   seed = 123         # Reproducibility )

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Bayesian G-Study
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: View modify default priors:
code: # View default priors for a model library(brms) default_prior(Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) +                 (1 | Person:Task),               data = brennan)  # Define custom priors my_prior <- c(   # Person variance: half-Cauchy with scale 1   set_prior("cauchy(0, 1)", class = "sd", group = "Person"),    # Task variance: exponential with rate 1   set_prior("exponential(1)", class = "sd", group = "Task"),    # Rater-within-Task variance: exponential with rate 0.5 (more spread)   set_prior("exponential(0.5)", class = "sd", group = "Rater:Task"),    # Person:Task interaction: exponential with rate 1   set_prior("exponential(1)", class = "sd", group = "Person:Task"),    # Residual: half-Cauchy with scale 1   set_prior("cauchy(0, 1)", class = "sigma") )  g_custom <- gstudy(   Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) +     (1 | Person:Task),   data = brennan,   backend = "brms",   prior = my_prior )

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Bayesian G-Study
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Check sensitivity prior choice: worked sensitivity analysis. chunk actually runs two Bayesian G-studies different priors standard deviation parameters shows posteriors respond. use small chains, iter, warmup example builds quickly; real sensitivity analysis use defaults. Reading comparison. Person variance dominant signal, priors give nearly identical posteriors — data overwhelm prior signal strong. Task variance smaller, can see priors pulling different directions: weakly informative cauchy(0, 5) allows probability mass larger values, concentrated exponential(1) shrinks estimates toward smaller values. Residual variance shows largest prior sensitivity least well-identified small dataset. lesson: always run prior sensitivity analysis smallest variance components, prior choice matters .
code: # Weakly informative prior prior_weak <- set_prior("cauchy(0, 5)", class = "sd") g_weak <- gstudy(   Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task),   data = brennan,   backend = "brms",   prior = prior_weak )  # More informative prior prior_info <- set_prior("exponential(1)", class = "sd") g_info <- gstudy(   Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task),   data = brennan,   backend = "brms",   prior = prior_info )  # Compare posterior means g_weak$variance_components$estimate g_info$variance_components$estimate # Fit two models with contrasting priors prior_weak_run <- set_prior("cauchy(0, 5)", class = "sd") g_weak_run <- gstudy(   Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task),   data = brennan,   backend = "brms",   prior = prior_weak_run,   chains = 2, iter = 1000, warmup = 500, seed = 123, cores = 1 ) #> Running previous_headings: Using the facet Package > D-Study: Computing Reliability Coefficients
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: decision study (D-study) framework established Cronbach, Gleser, Nanda, & Rajaratnam (1972), formalized variance components G-studies projected alternative measurement designs. work introduced concepts universe score variance error variance remain central D-study methodology. Brennan (2001) provided comprehensive treatment D-study optimization, including cost-constrained designs analytical solutions optimal sample sizes. text remains primary reference D-study methodology, covering univariate multivariate D-studies. Brennan & Kane (1977) introduced phi-cut coefficient criterion-referenced decisions, showing dependability depends distance mean cut score. work extended G-theory mastery testing competency-based assessment contexts. Kane & Brennan (1980) extended framework agreement-based indices domain-referenced tests, providing additional coefficients criterion-referenced interpretations. Marcoulides & Goldstein (1992) developed optimization procedures multivariate G-studies budget constraints, addressing practical question allocate resources across facets designing measurement studies. D-study uses G-study results compute reliability coefficients different measurement designs.

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > D-Study: Computing Reliability Coefficients
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: output shows: G coefficient: Generalizability coefficient relative decisions Phi coefficient: Dependability coefficient absolute decisions Reading G Phi values. Conventional thresholds educational psychological measurement: G ≥ 0.80 acceptable high-stakes decisions (selection, certification, promotion); G ≥ 0.70 acceptable medium-stakes decisions (formative feedback, progress monitoring); G ≥ 0.60 acceptable low-stakes research. Phi always less equal G, gap tells much absolute-error variance (.e., systematic facet effects like rater leniency item difficulty) contributing. small gap (e.g., G = 0.82, Phi = 0.80) means facets small main effects; large gap (e.g., G = 0.82, Phi = 0.55) means facets substantial main effects consider whether decisions need norm-referenced (use G) criterion-referenced (use Phi).
code: # Conduct D-study with 3 tasks and 4 raters d <- dstudy(g, n = list(Task = 3, Rater = 4))  print(d) #> Decision Study (D-Study) #> ======================== #>  #> Based on G-Study with lme4 backend #> Object of measurement: Person  #> Universe components: Person  #> Error components for relative error (sigma2_delta): Person:Task (Residual)  #> Error components for absolute error (sigma2_delta_abs): Task, Person:Task (Residual)  #>  #> Sample Sizes: #>  Task: 3 #>  Rater: 4 #>  #> Variance Components: #> # A tibble: 3 × 6 #>   component dim   var_unscaled pct_unscaled var_scaled pct_scaled #>   <chr>     <chr>        <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 Person    Score        0.584         13.3      0.584      31.5  #> 2 Task      Score        0.52          11.8      0.173       9.34 #> 3 Residual  Score        3.29          74.9      1.10       59.2  #>  #> Coefficients: #>    dim   uni sigma2_delta sigma2_delta_abs     g   phi #>  Score 0.584          1.1             1.27 0.347 0.315

text: criterion-referenced decisions, phi-cut coefficient incorporates specific cut-score: ϕcut=στ2+(μ−c)2στ2+σΔ2+(μ−c)2\phi_{cut} = \frac{\sigma^2_\tau + (\mu - c)^2}{\sigma^2_\tau + \sigma^2_\Delta + (\mu - c)^2} cc cut-score μ\mu grand mean. phi-cut coefficient useful decisions like “person exceed passing threshold?” phi-cut depends cut-score distance grand mean. cut-score equals grand mean, term (μ−c)2=0(\mu - c)^2 = 0 ϕcut\phi_{cut} reduces ordinary phi coefficient. cut-score moves away grand mean, (μ−c)2(\mu - c)^2 term grows, increases numerator denominator amount, proportional effect denominator (error variance + squared distance) larger error variance small. practical consequence: cut-score mean, conservative phi-cut becomes, difficult argue pass/fail decisions dependable. mastery testing extreme cut-scores (e.g., “must score ≥ 95%”) requires longer instruments pass/fail mean.
code: # Extract grand mean from G-study mu_y <- mean(brennan$Score) cat("Grand mean:", mu_y, "\n") #> Grand mean: 4.75  # D-study with cut-score of 7 d_cut <- dstudy(g, n = list(Task = 3, Rater = 4), cut_score = 7)  print(d_cut) #> Decision Study (D-Study) #> ======================== #>  #> Based on G-Study with lme4 backend #> Object of measurement: Person  #> Universe components: Person  #> Error components for relative error (sigma2_delta): Person:Task (Residual)  #> Error components for absolute error (sigma2_delta_abs): Task, Person:Task (Residual)  #>  #> Sample Sizes: #>  Task: 3 #>  Rater: 4 #>  #> Variance Components: #> # A tibble: 3 × 6 #>   component dim   var_unscaled pct_unscaled var_scaled pct_scaled #>   <chr>     <chr>        <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 Person    Score        0.584         13.3      0.584      31.5  #> 2 Task      Score        0.52          11.8      0.173       9.34 #> 3 Residual  Score        3.29          74.9      1.10       59.2  #>  #> Coefficients: #>    dim   uni sigma2_delta sigma2_delta_abs     g   phi phi_cut #>  Score 0.584          1.1             1.27 0.347 0.315   0.816

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Advanced D-Study Options
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: default, universe includes object measurement. can include additional components: useful facet considered “fixed” universe generalization. default, object measurement (typically Person) treated facet generalization — universe “admissible observations persons.” add Person:Task universe, saying: “person--task interaction part construct want generalize , error want minimize.” raises universe-score variance reliability, error variance correspondingly smaller. Use option interaction theoretically meaningful construct (e.g., person--task interaction clinical skills assessment reflects real, stable trait person performs across different tasks).
code: # Include Person:Task interaction in universe d_universe <- dstudy(   g,    n = list(Task = 3, Rater = 4),   universe = c("Person", "Person:Task") )  summary(d_universe) #> === D-Study Summary === #>  #> Object of measurement: Person  #> Based on G-Study: Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task)  #>  #> Sample Sizes: #>  Task: 3 #>  Rater: 4 #>  #> Variance Components: #> # A tibble: 3 × 6 #>   component dim   var_unscaled pct_unscaled var_scaled pct_scaled #>   <chr>     <chr>        <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 Person    Score        0.584         13.3      0.584      31.5  #> 2 Task      Score        0.52          11.8      0.173       9.34 #> 3 Residual  Score        3.29          74.9      1.10       59.2  #>  #> Coefficients: #>     dim   uni sigma2_delta sigma2_delta_abs         g      phi #> 1 Score 3.877     1.097667            1.271 0.7793487 0.753108

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Advanced D-Study Options
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: chunk runs D-study twice — default universe (Person ) Person:Task added — prints G Phi coefficients side--side effect choice concrete: Reading comparison. Adding Person:Task universe increases G (Phi) interaction variance moved “error” “signal.” mathematically correct conceptually loaded: means claiming way person responds differently different tasks part construct want measure, flaw measurement. Use default universe construct “person’s general ability, regardless task happened see” (typical G-theory assumption). Use custom universe theoretical empirical reasons believe interaction reflects stable, generalizable trait.
code: # Default universe: only Person is in the universe d_default <- dstudy(g, n = list(Task = 3, Rater = 4))  # Custom universe: Person + Person:Task d_custom <- dstudy(   g,   n = list(Task = 3, Rater = 4),   universe = c("Person", "Person:Task") )  # Side-by-side comparison tribble(   ~universe,                            ~G,    ~Phi,   "default (Person only)",              round(d_default$coefficients$g, 3),                                          round(d_default$coefficients$phi, 3),   "custom (Person + Person:Task)",      round(d_custom$coefficients$g, 3),                                          round(d_custom$coefficients$phi, 3) ) #> # A tibble: 2 × 3 #>   universe                          G   Phi #>   <chr>                         <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 default (Person only)         0.347 0.315 #> 2 custom (Person + Person:Task) 0.779 0.753

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Advanced D-Study Options
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Specify components contribute error: default, interaction effects involving object measurement treated error. error parameter lets restrict error specific interactions. example, evidence Person:Task random noise reflects stable person--task interaction, can exclude error (add universe shown ). Use error carefully: misclassifying real source variation error inflates error variance depresses G; misclassifying noise signal inflates universe-score variance overstates G.
code: # Only use specific interactions as error sources d_error <- dstudy(   g,   n = list(Task = 3, Rater = 4),   error = c("Person:Task", "Person:Rater") )  summary(d_error) #> === D-Study Summary === #>  #> Object of measurement: Person  #> Based on G-Study: Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task)  #>  #> Sample Sizes: #>  Task: 3 #>  Rater: 4 #>  #> Variance Components: #> # A tibble: 3 × 6 #>   component dim   var_unscaled pct_unscaled var_scaled pct_scaled #>   <chr>     <chr>        <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 Person    Score        0.584         13.3      0.584      31.5  #> 2 Task      Score        0.52          11.8      0.173       9.34 #> 3 Residual  Score        3.29          74.9      1.10       59.2  #>  #> Coefficients: #>     dim   uni sigma2_delta sigma2_delta_abs g phi #> 1 Score 0.584            0                0 1   1

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Advanced D-Study Options
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: averaging facet, use aggregation parameter: aggregation parameter tells facet plan average facet D-study rather treat separate dimension inference. example, 4 raters per task plan use mean 4 ratings score task, Rater “aggregation” facet. reliability mean n_r ratings depends rater variance component rater--person interaction. residual_is parameter tells facet call residual aggregation; example, residual becomes three-way Person:Task:Rater interaction. Use aggregation whenever D-study design involves taking means (e.g., mean k ratings, mean n items).
code: # Average scores across Raters d_agg <- dstudy(   g,   n = list(Task = 3, Rater = 4),   aggregation = "Rater",   residual_is = "Person:Task:Rater" )  summary(d_agg) #> === D-Study Summary === #>  #> Object of measurement: Person  #> Based on G-Study: Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task)  #>  #> Sample Sizes: #>  Task: 3 #>  Rater: 4 #>  #> Variance Components: #> # A tibble: 3 × 6 #>   component dim   var_unscaled pct_unscaled var_scaled pct_scaled #>   <chr>     <chr>        <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 Person    Score        0.584         13.3      0.584       56.6 #> 2 Task      Score        0.52          11.8      0.173       16.8 #> 3 Residual  Score        3.29          74.9      0.274       26.6 #>  #> Coefficients: #>     dim   uni sigma2_delta sigma2_delta_abs         g       phi #> 1 Score 0.584    0.2744167          0.44775 0.6803223 0.5660286

code: # Bayesian G-study g_brms <- gstudy(   Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task),   data = brennan,   backend = "brms" )  # D-study with 95% credible intervals d_ci <- dstudy(   g_brms,   n = list(Task = 3),   ci = c("g", "phi") )  print(d_ci)

previous_headings: Using the facet Package
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Data note. mvbind() requires wide-format data one row per (Person, Rater) cell one column per task/dimension. brennan dataset Rater-nested--Task, wide pivot missing values every row. illustrate mvbind() properly, examples use small synthetic fully crossed Person ×\times Task ×\times Rater design every rater rates every person every task. multiple outcome variables, use brms backend mvbind():
code: # Synthetic fully crossed design: 6 persons x 3 tasks x 4 raters set.seed(42) n_p <- 6; n_t <- 3; n_r <- 4 crossed_data <- expand.grid(   Person = paste0("P", seq_len(n_p)),   Task   = seq_len(n_t),   Rater  = paste0("R", seq_len(n_r)),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) mu <- 5 task_eff  <- c(0.5, 0, -0.5) rater_eff <- c(0.2, 0.1, -0.1, -0.2) p_eff <- setNames(rnorm(n_p, 0, 1), paste0("P", seq_len(n_p))) crossed_data$Score <- with(crossed_data,   mu + p_eff[Person] +     task_eff[Task] +     rater_eff[as.integer(sub("R", "", Rater))] +     rnorm(nrow(crossed_data), 0, 0.8)   # residual + interactions )  # Pivot to wide format: one row per Person x Rater, one column per Task crossed_wide <- crossed_data %>%   tidyr::pivot_wider(     names_from  = Task,     values_from = Score,     names_prefix = "Task"   )  head(crossed_wide) #   Person Rater Task1 Task2 Task3 # 1     P1    R1   8.3   5.5   4.1 # 2     P2    R1   5.1   4.4   5.2 # 3     P3    R1   7.7   5.5   4.8 # 4     P4    R1   6.3   6.3   3.9 # 5     P5    R1   7.2   5.4   5.0 # 6     P6    R1   7.4   3.0   5.6  # Multivariate G-study g_mv <- gstudy(   mvbind(Task1, Task2, Task3) ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Rater),   data = crossed_wide,   backend = "brms" )  summary(g_mv)

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Multivariate G-Study > Composite Coefficients
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: composite score formed weighted sum dimension scores: Ycomposite=∑dwdYdY_{composite} = \sum_d w_d Y_d variance composite variance component : σcomposite2=𝐰′𝚺𝐰=∑dwd2σd2+2∑d<d′wdwd′σdd′\sigma^2_{composite} = \mathbf{w}'\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} = \sum_d w_d^2 \sigma^2_d + 2\sum_{d<d'} w_d w_{d'} \sigma_{dd'} : 𝐰\mathbf{w} vector weights (one per dimension) 𝚺\boldsymbol{\Sigma} variance-covariance matrix component σd2\sigma^2_d diagonal elements (variances dimension) σdd′\sigma_{dd'} -diagonal elements (covariances dimensions) composite G Phi coefficients computed using composite variance components: Gcomposite=στ,composite2στ,composite2+σδ,composite2G_{composite} = \frac{\sigma^2_{\tau, composite}}{\sigma^2_{\tau, composite} + \sigma^2_{\delta, composite}} Φcomposite=στ,composite2στ,composite2+σΔ,composite2\Phi_{composite} = \frac{\sigma^2_{\tau, composite}}{\sigma^2_{\tau, composite} + \sigma^2_{\Delta, composite}}

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Multivariate G-Study > Composite Coefficients
what: Key Insight: The Role of Covariances
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: dimensions positively correlated, composite reliability benefits shared variance: Covariance terms (σdd′\sigma_{dd'}) contribute positively dimensions positively correlated composite reliability often exceeds average dimension-specific reliabilities composite “borrows strength” correlations dimensions example, two subscales measure related constructs persons score high one tend score high , composite score reliable either subscale alone correlation adds information. Composite variance worked example. Suppose two subscales, variance σ12=σ22=1.0\sigma^2_1 = \sigma^2_2 = 1.0, correlation subscales ρ12=0.5\rho_{12} = 0.5 (covariance σ12=0.5\sigma_{12} = 0.5). equal weights w=(0.5,0.5)w = (0.5, 0.5), composite variance : σcomposite2=w12σ12+w22σ22+2w1w2σ12=(0.25)(1)+(0.25)(1)+2(0.5)(0.5)(0.5)=0.75\sigma^2_{composite} = w_1^2 \sigma^2_1 + w_2^2 \sigma^2_2 + 2 w_1 w_2 \sigma_{12} = (0.25)(1) + (0.25)(1) + 2(0.5)(0.5)(0.5) = 0.75 Without covariance term, 0.50 — correlation adds 50% variance composite. mathematical basis “composite reliability often exceeds average dimension reliabilities” claim: cross-product term 2w1w2σ122 w_1 w_2 \sigma_{12} contributes positively subscales positively correlated. Conversely, subscales negatively correlated (rare theoretically possible situation), composite variance smaller average dimension variances — composite reliability lower. essential inspect residual correlation matrix multivariate G-study object forming composite: sign magnitude correlations determine whether combining subscales helps hurts.

title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: default, dstudy() uses equal weights (1 dimension): output shows coefficients dimension plus “Composite” row:
code: # Multivariate G-study on the fully crossed synthetic data g_mv <- gstudy(   mvbind(Task1, Task2, Task3) ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Rater),   data = crossed_wide,   backend = "brms" )  # D-study with default equal weights d_mv <- dstudy(g_mv, n = list(Rater = 4))  # View coefficients (includes Composite row) print(d_mv) Dimension: Task1 ----------------------------------------   Rater    uni sigma2_delta sigma2_delta_abs     g   phi       4 0.5234       0.1234           0.1876 0.809 0.736  Dimension: Task2 ----------------------------------------   Rater    uni sigma2_delta sigma2_delta_abs     g   phi       4 0.4876       0.1456           0.2034 0.770 0.706  Dimension: Task3 ----------------------------------------   Rater    uni sigma2_delta sigma2_delta_abs     g   phi       4 0.5123       0.1345           0.1923 0.792 0.727  Composite (weights: 1, 1, 1) ----------------------------------------   Rater    uni sigma2_delta sigma2_delta_abs     g   phi       4 1.5234       0.3890           0.5789 0.797 0.725

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Multivariate G-Study > Composite Coefficients

text: Wide Format (mvbind) — dimension separate column. format requires fully crossed design every (Person, Rater) cell value every task: Long Format (bf) — dimensions identified grouping variable: Key Differences:
code: # Fully crossed synthetic data (defined in the Multivariate G-Study section) library(tidyr) crossed_wide <- crossed_data %>%   pivot_wider(     names_from = Task,     values_from = Score,     names_prefix = "Task"   )  head(crossed_wide) #   Person Rater Task1 Task2 Task3 # 1     P1    R1   8.3   5.5   4.1 # 2     P2    R1   5.1   4.4   5.2 # 3     P3    R1   7.7   5.5   4.8 # 4     P4    R1   6.3   6.3   3.9 # 5     P5    R1   7.2   5.4   5.0 # 6     P6    R1   7.4   3.0   5.6  # Multivariate G-study g_mv_wide <- gstudy(   mvbind(Task1, Task2, Task3) ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Rater),   data = crossed_wide,   backend = "brms" ) # Use rajaratnam dataset (nested: items within subtests) data(rajaratnam)  head(rajaratnam) #   Person Subtest Item Score # 1      1       1    1     4 # 2      1       1    2     5 # ...  # Multivariate G-study with long format g_mv_long <- gstudy(   bf(Score ~ 0 + Subtest +          (0 + Subtest | r | Person) +          (0 + Subtest || Item)),   sigma ~ 0 + Subtest,   data = rajaratnam,   backend = "brms" )
