previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Multivariate G-Study
what: Wide vs Long Format: A Worked Comparison
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: two formats interchangeable — wide format requires fully crossed design (one value per cell) uses simpler syntax, long format option unbalanced data gives modeling flexibility. chunk uses synthetic fully crossed crossed_data / crossed_wide defined Multivariate G-Study section: Reading comparison. data balanced (brennan ), wide format natural choice: simpler syntax, faster fit, gives residual correlations dimensions free. data unbalanced — example, persons Task1 Task2 Task3, items nested within subtests different numbers items per subtest — wide format viable, mvbind() requires complete cases. two paths forward: (1) stay wide format mom backend passing unbalanced = TRUE gstudy(). Mom uses Henderson’s Method III, pairwise complete cases used correlation matrix; per-dimension totals preserved surfaced gstudy_obj$n_per_dim. can pass per-dimension n tibble dstudy() (columns dim, facet, n) facet g phi coefficients computed per-dimension correct divisor. (2) reshape long format brms backend use bf() syntax. also handles nesting lets model dimension-specific residual variances (sigma ~ 0 + Subtest). Use wide format mom + unbalanced = TRUE want simplest code path accept Henderson-style estimation; use long format brms need full Bayesian inference missingness non-Gaussian residuals.
code: # Wide format: one column per dimension crossed_wide <- crossed_data %>%   tidyr::pivot_wider(     names_from = Task,     values_from = Score,     names_prefix = "Task"   )  # Long format on a multivariate variable: each row is a (person, task) score # and the multivariate response is built from the columns of crossed_data. # (The brms bf() syntax is more complex; see Multivariate G-Study section # for the full multivariate model.)  # Wide-format G-study (default for multivariate) g_wide_run <- gstudy(   mvbind(Task1, Task2, Task3) ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Rater),   data = crossed_wide,   backend = "brms",   chains = 2, iter = 1000, warmup = 500, seed = 123, cores = 1 )  # Print the wide-format variance components tidy(g_wide_run)

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Value Added Ratio (VAR)
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Value Added Ratio developed Haberman (2008) address fundamental question psychological educational measurement: subscores add value total composite score? question critical test developers deciding whether report subscale scores alongside composite scores. Vispoel, Lee, Hong, & Chen (2023) extended framework multivariate generalizability theory, providing comprehensive approach evaluating subscale viability within G-theory framework. work demonstrates VAR can computed relative (rank-ordering) absolute (domain-referenced) interpretations.

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Value Added Ratio (VAR)
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: test contains multiple subscales, researchers often face practical dilemma: subscale scores reported separately, composite (total) score used? Traditional approaches compare subscale reliability composite reliability, comparison incomplete ignores information provided correlations subscales. Value Added Ratio (VAR) quantifies whether subscale’s scores better estimate universe score obtained projecting composite score onto subscale. Specifically: VAR > 1: subscale’s scores provide better estimate universe score composite . Reporting subscale score separately adds value. VAR ≈\approx 1: subscale composite provide equally good estimates. Either approach defensible. VAR < 1: composite provides better estimate subscale’s universe score subscale’s scores. Reporting composite recommended.

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Value Added Ratio (VAR)
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: VAR subscale SiS_i defined : VAR(Si)=PRMSE(Si)PRMSE(C→Si)VAR(S_i) = \frac{PRMSE(S_i)}{PRMSE(C \rightarrow S_i)} : PRMSE(Si)PRMSE(S_i) proportional reduction mean squared error using subscale’s scores estimate universe score (equals subscale’s G Phi coefficient) PRMSE(C→Si)PRMSE(C \rightarrow S_i) proportional reduction mean squared error using composite score estimate subscale’s universe score key innovation Haberman formula PRMSE(C→Si)PRMSE(C \rightarrow S_i) incorporates covariances subscales: PRMSE(C→Si)=[Cov(τi,C)]2Var(τi)⋅Rel(C)⋅Var(C)PRMSE(C \rightarrow S_i) = \frac{[\mathrm{Cov}(\tau_i, C)]^2}{\mathrm{Var}(\tau_i) \cdot \mathrm{Rel}(C) \cdot \mathrm{Var}(C)} Cov(τi,C)=∑jwjCov(τi,τj)\mathrm{Cov}(\tau_i, C) = \sum_j w_j \, \mathrm{Cov}(\tau_i, \tau_j) weighted covariance subscale ii’s universe score composite universe score, computed universe-score (disattenuated) covariance matrix Στ\Sigma_\tau. : Var(τi)=Στ,ii\mathrm{Var}(\tau_i) = \Sigma_{\tau,ii} universe-score variance subscale ii Rel(C)=Var(τC)/Var(C)\mathrm{Rel}(C) = \mathrm{Var}(\tau_C) / \mathrm{Var}(C) composite reliability (G relative, Φ\Phi absolute) Var(C)=𝐰⊤Σobs𝐰\mathrm{Var}(C) = \mathbf{w}^\top \Sigma_{\mathrm{obs}} \mathbf{w} D-study observed variance composite 𝐰\mathbf{w} vector subscale weights (e.g., wj=1/nsw_j = 1/n_s equal weighting) Note Vispoel et al. (2023) Equation 38. formula Haberman (2008) correct form. equation labeled “Equation 38” Vispoel et al. (2023, p. 9) contains typesetting error denominator (using σ̂2(Si)⋅Rel(C)\hat\sigma^2(S_i) \cdot \mathrm{Rel}(C) instead Var(τi)⋅Rel(C)\mathrm{Var}(\tau_i) \cdot \mathrm{Rel}(C)); implementation facet::prmse() uses corrected form, matches numerical values reported Table 8. Key insight: subscales positively correlated, covariance terms (Cov(τi,τj)\mathrm{Cov}(\tau_i, \tau_j)) increase |Cov(τi,C)||\mathrm{Cov}(\tau_i, C)|, making likely composite provides useful information estimating subscale’s universe score. VAR can less 1 even subscale reliability high—strong correlations subscales mean composite “borrows strength” relationships.

text: prmse() function computes three distinct types PRMSE, answering different question prediction accuracy: addition, global profile reliability available attr(result, "prmse_mv_rel"), trace matrix (diagonal slice), normalized tr(Στ)\mathrm{tr}(\Sigma_\tau). equals matrix analog squared canonical correlation always satisfies PRMSEMV≥PRMSEP[d]\mathrm{PRMSE}_{MV} \geq \mathrm{PRMSE}_P[d] dimension dd.

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Value Added Ratio (VAR)
what: Validating the PRMSE formula on synthetic data
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Haberman (2008) closed form fully reproducible package’s output. following example constructs balanced 3-dimension design known variance components shows prmse() recovers analytical values within ANOVA estimation noise. pkg_prmse_* values match ref_prmse_* within 0.05 (tolerance ANOVA estimation noise chosen sample sizes). validation also encoded package’s automated test suite (tests/testthat/test-viable.R, tests H1–H10).
code: library(facet) set.seed(123) n_persons <- 1500 n_items <- 10  # True data-generating parameters: #   Var(tau_A) = Var(tau_B) = Var(tau_C) = 1.0 #   Cov(tau_A, tau_B) = Cov(tau_A, tau_C) = Cov(tau_B, tau_C) = 0.5 #   Item variance = 0.1 per subscale #   Person:Item variance = 0.9 per subscale Sigma_person <- matrix(c(1, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 1, 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 1), 3, 3,   dimnames = list(c("A", "B", "C"), c("A", "B", "C")))  # Generate Person effects and build the data L <- chol(Sigma_person) tau <- matrix(rnorm(n_persons * 3), n_persons, 3) %*% L data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_len(n_persons), function(p) {   data.frame(     person = factor(p),     item   = factor(seq_len(n_items)),     A = tau[p, 1] + rnorm(n_items, 0, sqrt(0.1)) + rnorm(n_items, 0, sqrt(0.9)),     B = tau[p, 2] + rnorm(n_items, 0, sqrt(0.1)) + rnorm(n_items, 0, sqrt(0.9)),     C = tau[p, 3] + rnorm(n_items, 0, sqrt(0.1)) + rnorm(n_items, 0, sqrt(0.9))   ) }))  # Fit and run prmse g <- gstudy(mvbind(A, B, C) ~ (1 | person) + (1 | item), data = data, backend = "mom") d <- dstudy(g, n = list(item = 10), weights = c(A = 1, B = 1, C = 1)) result <- suppressWarnings(prmse(d))  # Closed-form reference (Haberman 2008) n_i <- 10 Sigma_tau <- Sigma_person Sigma_obs_rel <- Sigma_tau diag(Sigma_obs_rel) <- diag(Sigma_obs_rel) + 0.9 / n_i w <- rep(1/3, 3) var_tau_C <- as.numeric(t(w) %*% Sigma_tau %*% w) var_obs_C_rel <- as.numeric(t(w) %*% Sigma_obs_rel %*% w) Rel_C <- var_tau_C / var_obs_C_rel cov_tau_C <- as.numeric(Sigma_tau %*% w)  # PRMSE_S reference = G coefficient ref_s <- diag(Sigma_tau) / (diag(Sigma_tau) + 0.9 / n_i)  # PRMSE_C reference = Haberman closed form ref_c <- (cov_tau_C^2) / (diag(Sigma_tau) * Rel_C * var_obs_C_rel)  # Compare data.frame(   dim = c("A", "B", "C"),   ref_prmse_s = ref_s,   pkg_prmse_s = result$prmse_s_rel,   ref_prmse_c = ref_c,   pkg_prmse_c = result$prmse_c_rel )

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Value Added Ratio (VAR)
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: facet package computes two VAR measures: var_rel: Based G coefficients (relative decisions). Use purpose rank-ordering persons making relative comparisons. var_abs: Based Phi coefficients (absolute decisions). Use purpose comparing scores standard cut-score. practice, var_abs typically lower var_rel absolute error includes variance components, making subscale-specific estimation challenging relative composite.

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Value Added Ratio (VAR)
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: VAR requires multivariate data multiple dimensions. following example uses rajaratnam dataset, three subtests: output shows PRMSE VAR subscale: call can pivoted show VAR columns first:
code: library(facet) library(brms)  # Multivariate G-study with items nested within subtests # Note: This uses long-format specification g_mv <- gstudy(   bf(Score ~ 0 + Subtest + (0 + Subtest | r | Person) + (0 + Subtest || Item),      sigma ~ 0 + Subtest),   data = rajaratnam,   backend = "brms",   chains = 2,   iter = 1000 )  # D-study computing VAR d_mv <- dstudy(   g_mv,   n = list(Person = 5) )  # Access PRMSE and VAR results using prmse() prmse(d_mv) # A tibble: 3 x 13   dim prmse_c_rel prmse_c_rel_LL prmse_c_rel_UL prmse_c_abs prmse_c_abs_LL prmse_c_abs_UL   <chr> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> 1 1 1.12 0.98 1.28 0.98 0.84 1.12 2 2 0.95 0.82 1.08 0.84 0.72 0.96 3 3 1.08 0.92 1.24 0.91 0.78 1.04 # … with 6 more variables: var_rel, var_rel_LL, var_rel_UL, # var_abs, var_abs_LL, var_abs_UL # A tibble: 3 x 13   dim   var_rel var_rel_LL var_rel_UL var_abs var_abs_LL var_abs_UL   <chr>   <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl>   <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl> 1 1        1.12       0.98       1.28    0.98       0.84       1.12 2 2        0.95       0.82       1.08    0.84       0.72       0.96 3 3        1.08       0.92       1.24    0.91       0.78       1.04 # … with 6 more variables: prmse_c_rel, prmse_c_rel_LL, prmse_c_rel_UL, #   prmse_c_abs, prmse_c_abs_LL, prmse_c_abs_UL

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Value Added Ratio (VAR)
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: VAR results stored var element dstudy object can accessed several ways:
code: # Get a tidy data frame with all PRMSE and VAR results prmse(d_mv)  # Access posterior means directly (use [[ to avoid partial matching with variance_components) d_mv[["var"]]$var_rel     # VAR (relative) means d_mv[["var"]]$var_abs     # VAR (absolute) means d_mv[["var"]]$prmse_c_rel # PRMSE(C->S_i) (relative) means d_mv[["var"]]$prmse_c_abs # PRMSE(C->S_i) (absolute) means  # Access full posterior draws (for custom analysis) dim(d_mv[["var"]]$var_rel_draws)     # [n_draws, n_dims] dim(d_mv[["var"]]$prmse_c_rel_draws) # [n_draws, n_dims]

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Value Added Ratio (VAR)
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: using Bayesian estimation (backend = "brms"), VAR results include full posterior distributions. can compute credible intervals probability level: prmse() function returns: prmse_c_rel_LL, prmse_c_rel_UL: Lower upper limits PRMSE(C->S_i) (relative) prmse_c_abs_LL, prmse_c_abs_UL: Lower upper limits PRMSE(C->S_i) (absolute) var_rel_LL, var_rel_UL: Lower upper limits VAR (relative) var_abs_LL, var_abs_UL: Lower upper limits VAR (absolute)
code: # Default 95% credible intervals prmse(d_mv)  # Custom credible intervals (e.g., 90%) prmse(d_mv, probs = c(0.05, 0.95))

text: Practical considerations: Sample size: VAR estimates stable larger samples. Use credible intervals assess precision. Correlation structure: Highly correlated subscales tend lower VAR values composite “borrows strength” correlations. Validity considerations: Even VAR > 1, subscale scores may worth reporting lack practical theoretical significance. Decision type: Consider whether decisions relative (use var_rel) absolute (use var_abs).

previous_headings: Using the facet Package
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: latent() function extracts universe score estimates based random effects: latent scores represent. values returned latent() estimates person’s universe score — score receive average across entire universe admissible observations (possible items, raters, occasions, etc., sampled population represented G-study). Two important properties: (1) latent scores shrunk toward grand mean compared raw person means — shrinkage larger persons fewer observations extreme observed scores, (2) latent scores scale original data, can interpret directly. Use “best estimate” person standing, raw person means, especially unbalanced observations per person.
code: # Basic latent scores (Person only) latent_scores <- latent(g)  head(latent_scores) #>   Person        latent #> 1      1  8.634646e-16 #> 2      2  6.801873e-01 #> 3      3 -5.668228e-01 #> 4      4 -5.668228e-01 #> 5      5  6.235050e-01 #> 6      6 -8.502341e-01

title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Visualize random effects (BLUPs) uncertainty:   Reading caterpillar plots. row one level facet (e.g., one rater, one task); point BLUP (best linear unbiased predictor) random effect, horizontal bar 95% confidence interval. Three patterns look : (1) levels whose CIs exclude zero — levels behave differently average (e.g., lenient rater, hard item); (2) CIs overlap heavily — levels meaningfully different one another; (3) asymmetry one direction — rater effects zero, average rater lenient grand mean suggest, signal check rater training issues. plot sorted effect size, extreme levels top bottom.
code: # All random effects plot_ranef(g) # Specific facet plot_ranef(g, which = "Person")

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Handling Estimation Issues
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Check convergence issues: singular fit. singular fit means one variance components estimated (near) zero, optimizer move away boundary. Three remedies: (1) drop offending facet formula theoretically unimportant; (2) switch backend = "mom" get positive truncated estimate, often interpretable practice (acknowledges component small positive); (3) switch backend = "brms" slightly informative prior exponential(1) SD — regularizes estimate away exactly zero. Singular fits necessarily problem can defend model without component, silently ignored.
code: # Check for warnings g$model@optinfo$conv$lme4 #> list()  # Singular fits indicate near-zero variance components lme4::isSingular(g$model) #> [1] FALSE

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Handling Estimation Issues
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Method moments can produce negative estimates, truncated zero: many components truncated. two variance components estimated exactly zero, design underpowered facets ’ve included — data simply don’t enough information distinguish sources variation noise. Remedies: (1) collect data, especially levels facets truncated; (2) reduce number facets model aggregating theoretically unimportant ones; (3) switch REML Bayesian — constrained non-negative estimates may give small positive values useful planning. single component truncated, often sign facet genuinely unimportant context, can drop design going forward.
code: # Check if any components were truncated g_mom$variance_components %>%   filter(var == 0) #> # A tibble: 0 × 8 #> # ℹ 8 variables: component <chr>, dim <chr>, type <chr>, var <dbl>, pct <dbl>, #> #   lower <dbl>, upper <dbl>, se <dbl>

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Handling Estimation Issues
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Diagnosing fixing convergence problems. R-hat > 1.05 component: () increase iter (try 8000+) warmup (try 2000+); (b) check model misspecification — missing interaction, wrongly crossed/nested term, unusual data pattern; (c) consider manually specifying bf() formula bf(y ~ ..., sigma ~ ...) control parameterization. Bulk_ESS < 400: try fixes, plus thin = 2 reduce autocorrelation chains. one component poor diagnostics (common pattern component small near zero), variance estimate may still approximately correct, credible interval unreliable; case, re-run stronger priors keep component away boundary (e.g., exponential(1) instead default Student-t).
code: # Check Rhat values g_bayes$variance_components %>%   select(component, Rhat, Bulk_ESS, Tail_ESS) %>%   mutate(     converged = Rhat < 1.01,     sufficient_ess = Bulk_ESS > 400 & Tail_ESS > 400   )  # View estimation issues g_bayes$estimation_issues

previous_headings: Using the facet Package
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: end--end analysis using brennan dataset. Note: Brennan dataset, Raters nested within Task, use nested notation Rater:Task:
code: library(facet) library(dplyr) library(tidyr) library(broom)  # 1. Load and explore data data(brennan) summary(brennan) #>  Task       Person       Rater        Score            y        #>  1:40   1      :12   1      :10   Min.   :1.00   Min.   :1.00   #>  2:40   2      :12   2      :10   1st Qu.:3.00   1st Qu.:3.00   #>  3:40   3      :12   3      :10   Median :5.00   Median :5.00   #>         4      :12   4      :10   Mean   :4.75   Mean   :4.75   #>         5      :12   5      :10   3rd Qu.:6.00   3rd Qu.:6.00   #>         6      :12   6      :10   Max.   :9.00   Max.   :9.00   #>         (Other):48   (Other):60  # Check design structure (Raters nested within Task) brennan %>%   group_by(Task) %>%   summarise(     raters = paste(sort(unique(Rater)), collapse = ", "),     n_raters = n_distinct(Rater)   ) #> # A tibble: 3 × 3 #>   Task  raters        n_raters #>   <fct> <chr>            <int> #> 1 1     1, 2, 3, 4           4 #> 2 2     5, 6, 7, 8           4 #> 3 3     9, 10, 11, 12        4  # 2. Conduct G-study with profile CIs (using nested design) g_full <- gstudy(   Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) +     (1 | Person:Task),   data = brennan,   ci_method = "profile" )  # 3. Examine variance components summary(g_full) #> === G-Study Summary === #>  #> Design Information: #>  Backend: lme4  #>  Formula: Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) + (1 | Person:Task)  #>  Observations: 120  #>  Multivariate: No  #>  #> Facet Information: #>  Object of measurement: Person  #>  Facets: Person, Task, Rater  #>  #> Sample Sizes: #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   effect             type            n #>   <chr>              <chr>       <dbl> #> 1 Person             main           10 #> 2 Task               main            3 #> 3 Rater:Task         interaction    12 #> 4 Person:Task        interaction    30 #> 5 Person:Rater (res) residual      120 #> 6 Rater per Task     nested          4 #>  #> Nested details: #>   Rater per Task: 3 Tasks, 4.0 Raters per Task #>  #> Variance Components: #> # A tibble: 5 × 5 #>   component   estimate   pct `2.5% CI` `97.5% CI` #>   <chr>          <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 Person         0.473 10.8      0           1.94 #> 2 Task           0.325  7.42     0           3.56 #> 3 Rater:Task     0.647 14.8      0.149       2.28 #> 4 Person:Task    0.56  12.8      0           1.88 #> 5 Residual       2.38  54.3      1.78        3.29 #>  #> Total variance: 4.385  # Identify largest sources of variance tidy(g_full) %>%   arrange(desc(var)) %>%   mutate(     cum_pct = cumsum(pct),     component = factor(component, levels = component)   ) #> # A tibble: 5 × 8 #>   component   dim   type          var   pct lower upper cum_pct #>   <fct>       <chr> <chr>       <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>   <dbl> #> 1 Residual    Score residual    2.38  54.3  1.78   3.29    54.3 #> 2 Rater:Task  Score interaction 0.647 14.8  0.149  2.28    69.0 #> 3 Person:Task Score interaction 0.56  12.8  0      1.88    81.8 #> 4 Person      Score main        0.473 10.8  0      1.94    92.6 #> 5 Task        Score main        0.325  7.42 0      3.56   100.  # 4. Conduct D-study to optimize design # Current design: 3 tasks, 4 raters per task d_current <- dstudy(g_full, n = list(Task = 3, `Rater:Task` = 4)) cat("Current design G:", d_current$coefficients$g, "\n") #> Current design G: 0.5512821 cat("Current design Phi:", d_current$coefficients$phi, "\n") #> Current design Phi: 0.4001692  # 5. Explore alternative designs d_explore <- dstudy(   g_full,   n = list(     Task = c(1, 2, 3, 5, 10),     `Rater:Task` = c(1, 2, 4, 8, 12)   ) )  # Find designs achieving G >= 0.80 tidy(d_explore) %>%   filter(g >= 0.80) %>%   arrange(g) #> # A tibble: 3 × 10 #>    Task `Rater:Task` dim     uni sigma2_delta sigma2_delta_abs     g   phi #>   <dbl>        <dbl> <chr> <dbl>        <dbl>            <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1    10            4 Score 0.473       0.116             0.213 0.804 0.690 #> 2    10            8 Score 0.473       0.0858            0.183 0.847 0.721 #> 3    10           12 Score 0.473       0.0758            0.173 0.862 0.732 #> # ℹ 2 more variables: sem_rel <dbl>, sem_abs <dbl>  # 6. Visualize plot(d_explore, type = "sweep") # 7. Make recommendation # Optimal design: minimum resources for G >= 0.80 optimal <- tidy(d_explore) %>%   filter(g >= 0.80) %>%   arrange(Task * `Rater:Task`) %>%   slice(1)  print(optimal) #> # A tibble: 1 × 10 #>    Task `Rater:Task` dim     uni sigma2_delta sigma2_delta_abs     g   phi #>   <dbl>        <dbl> <chr> <dbl>        <dbl>            <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> #> 1    10            4 Score 0.473        0.116            0.213 0.804 0.690 #> # ℹ 2 more variables: sem_rel <dbl>, sem_abs <dbl>

title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: complete example nested design (Raters nested within Task): Reading comparison. 5-task × 2-rater design achieves comparable higher G 3-task × 4-rater design (10 total observation cells vs 12), one fewer level per task one task overall. reason: dataset, Task facet variance Rater:Task facet, adding tasks reduces error faster adding raters . general lesson: sweep shows one facet contributes disproportionately error, investing levels facet cost-effective investing facets small variance components. Always inspect tidy(d_sweep) look facet largest ratio variance current n deciding spend next round data collection.
code: # G-study with nested design (Raters nested within Task) g_3f <- gstudy(   Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) +     (1 | Person:Task),   data = brennan )  summary(g_3f) #> === G-Study Summary === #>  #> Design Information: #>  Backend: lme4  #>  Formula: Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) + (1 | Person:Task)  #>  Observations: 120  #>  Multivariate: No  #>  #> Facet Information: #>  Object of measurement: Person  #>  Facets: Person, Task, Rater  #>  #> Sample Sizes: #> # A tibble: 6 × 3 #>   effect             type            n #>   <chr>              <chr>       <dbl> #> 1 Person             main           10 #> 2 Task               main            3 #> 3 Rater:Task         interaction    12 #> 4 Person:Task        interaction    30 #> 5 Person:Rater (res) residual      120 #> 6 Rater per Task     nested          4 #>  #> Nested details: #>   Rater per Task: 3 Tasks, 4.0 Raters per Task #>  #> Variance Components: #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>   component   estimate   pct #>   <chr>          <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 Person         0.473 10.8  #> 2 Task           0.325  7.42 #> 3 Rater:Task     0.647 14.8  #> 4 Person:Task    0.56  12.8  #> 5 Residual       2.38  54.3  #>  #> Total variance: 4.385  # D-study: what if we used 5 tasks with 2 raters each? # Note: Use backticks for nested facet names in dstudy d_3f <- dstudy(g_3f, n = list(Task = 5, `Rater:Task` = 2))  summary(d_3f) #> === D-Study Summary === #>  #> Object of measurement: Person  #> Based on G-Study: Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) + (1 | Person:Task)  #>  #> Sample Sizes: #>  Task: 5 #>  Rater:Task: 2 #>  #> Variance Components: #> # A tibble: 5 × 6 #>   component   dim   var_unscaled pct_unscaled var_scaled pct_scaled #>   <chr>       <chr>        <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 Person      Score        0.473        10.8       0.473      46.5  #> 2 Task        Score        0.325         7.41      0.065       6.39 #> 3 Rater:Task  Score        0.647        14.8       0.129      12.7  #> 4 Person:Task Score        0.56         12.8       0.112      11.0  #> 5 Residual    Score        2.38         54.3       0.238      23.4  #>  #> Coefficients: #>     dim   uni sigma2_delta sigma2_delta_abs         g       phi #> 1 Score 0.473         0.35           0.5444 0.5747266 0.4649106  # Compare with original design d_orig <- dstudy(g_3f, n = list(Task = 3, `Rater:Task` = 4))  cat("Original (3 tasks, 4 raters/task): G =", d_orig$coefficients$g, "\n") #> Original (3 tasks, 4 raters/task): G = 0.5512821 cat("Alternative (5 tasks, 2 raters/task): G =", d_3f$coefficients$g, "\n") #> Alternative (5 tasks, 2 raters/task): G = 0.5747266

title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: test contains multiple subscales, researchers often face fundamental tension: weights maximize composite reliability often differ weights maximize subscale viability (VAR). prmse() function provides three optimization methods find optimal weights different objectives.

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Weight Optimization for Composite Scores
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: conflict arises subscales reliable highly correlated others. high reliability suggests upweighting composite reliability, high correlation means contribute less unique information (lower VAR).

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Weight Optimization for Composite Scores
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: "composite" method finds weights maximize reliability composite score . uses analytical solution via generalized eigenvalue decomposition. use: Selection/norm-referencing decisions overall reliability paramount.
code: # Optimize weights for maximum composite reliability result <- prmse(d_mv, optimize = "composite")  # View optimal weights result$weights  # View composite reliability achieved result$composite_reliability

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Weight Optimization for Composite Scores
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: "subscale" method finds weights maximize VAR subscales. returns : Per-subscale optimizations: Weights maximize VAR specific subscale Minimax optimization: Weights maximize minimum VAR across subscales use: Profile interpretation subscales matter equally.
code: # Optimize weights for maximum subscale VAR result <- prmse(d_mv, optimize = "subscale")  # View minimax results (maximizes minimum VAR) result$minimax$weights result$minimax$min_var  # View per-subscale results result$per_subscale  # View comparison table result$comparison

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Weight Optimization for Composite Scores
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: "tuning" method performs exhaustive grid search weight combinations. useful diagnostic purposes understanding weight-VAR relationship. use: Diagnostic purposes understanding weight-VAR relationships.
code: # Grid search with 0.2 resolution result <- prmse(d_mv, optimize = "tuning", grid_resolution = 0.2)  # View best solution result$best$weights  # View full grid results head(result$grid_results)  # Number of viable solutions (all VAR > 1) result$n_viable

previous_headings: Using the facet Package > Weight Optimization for Composite Scores
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: three optimization methods often give different weights data, optimize different objectives. chunk fits three prints side--side comparison: Reading comparison. composite method puts weight reliable, highly correlated subscales — “borrow strength” . subscale tuning methods spread weight evenly (toward less-correlated subscales) keep minimum VAR across subscales high. “right” choice depends decision context: selection (e.g., admit top 20%) cares composite reliability, use composite; profile interpretation (e.g., school report showing strengths weaknesses across subjects) needs subscales useful, use subscale. two objectives conflict strongly, useful signal subscales redundant (consider dropping one) unreliable (consider adding items).
code: # Fit all three methods on the same multivariate D-study comp <- prmse(d_mv, optimize = "composite") sub  <- prmse(d_mv, optimize = "subscale") tun  <- prmse(d_mv, optimize = "tuning", grid_resolution = 0.1)  # Build a small comparison tibble tribble(   ~method,        ~weights,                            ~objective_value,    ~objective_name,   "composite",    round(comp$weights, 3),             round(comp$composite_reliability, 3),  "composite reliability",   "subscale (minimax)", round(sub$minimax$weights, 3), round(sub$minimax$min_var, 3),       "min VAR across subscales",   "tuning",       round(tun$best$weights, 3),         round(tun$best$min_var, 3),           "min VAR (grid search)" )

previous_headings: Reporting Guidelines > Reporting Bayesian G-Theory
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Bayesian Estimation: Variance components estimated using Bayesian methods via brms package (Bürkner 2017) R, implements Hamiltonian Monte Carlo sampling Stan (Carpenter et al. 2017). specified weakly informative half-Cauchy(0, 2) priors variance components (Gelman 2006), reflecting minimal prior information ensuring proper posterior concentration. Four chains run 4,000 iterations (2,000 warmup), yielding 8,000 posterior samples inference. Convergence assessed using Gelman-Rubin statistic (R-hat < 1.01) effective sample size (ESS > 400; (Vehtari et al. 2021)). Prior Specification: conducted prior sensitivity analyses comparing results weakly informative [specify prior] informative [specify prior] priors. Posterior estimates robust prior specification, differences variance component estimates < [X]% across prior choices.

previous_headings: Reporting Guidelines > Reporting Bayesian G-Theory
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Variance Components: Table [X] presents posterior summaries variance components. Person variance accounted largest proportion total variance (posterior median = [X], 95% CI = [X, X]), indicating substantial individual differences [construct]. [facet] variance component smaller (posterior median = [X], 95% CI = [X, X]), suggesting [interpretation]. Reliability Coefficients: design [n] [facet]s, generalizability coefficient [X] (95% CI = [X, X]), indicating [interpretation] reliability relative decisions. dependability coefficient [X] (95% CI = [X, X]) absolute decisions. Multivariate Results: multivariate model, dimension-specific reliabilities ranged [X] [X] (Table [X]). composite reliability [X] (95% CI = [X, X]) equal weights. Value Added Ratios indicated [subscale name] (VAR = [X], 95% CI = [X, X]) [adds value / provides added value] beyond composite.

previous_headings: Reporting Guidelines > APA-Style Tables
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Table X. Bayesian Variance Component Estimates Note. SD = Posterior standard deviation; CI = Credible interval; ESS = Effective sample size.
code: # Function to create APA-style table for variance components make_apa_variance_table <- function(gstudy_obj) {   vc <- gstudy_obj$variance_components    # Create formatted table   data.frame(     Source = vc$component,     Estimate = sprintf("%.3f", vc$estimate),     SD = sprintf("%.3f", vc$sd),     `95% CI` = sprintf("[%.3f, %.3f]", vc$lower, vc$upper),     Rhat = sprintf("%.3f", vc$Rhat),     ESS = round(vc$Bulk_ESS)   ) }

previous_headings: Exercises > Computational Exercises
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Using brennan dataset (Rater nested within Task): Fit Bayesian G-study Person, Task, Rater:Task facets using default priors. Report posterior means, 95% credible intervals, convergence diagnostics. Compare results frequentist REML estimate. interval estimates differ?
code: library(facet) data(brennan)  # a) Bayesian G-study g_bayes <- gstudy(   Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) +     (1 | Person:Task),   data = brennan,   backend = "brms" )  # b) Posterior summaries print(g_bayes)  # c) Compare to REML g_reml <- gstudy(   Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) +     (1 | Person:Task),   data = brennan,   backend = "lme4" )  # Compare estimates g_bayes$variance_components$estimate g_reml$variance_components$var

previous_headings: Exercises > Computational Exercises
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Define custom priors encode prior information: “Person variance expected moderate (around 1.0 score scale) uncertainty.” Fit G-study priors compare default priors. Conduct prior sensitivity analysis varying prior scale parameter.
code: library(brms)  # a) Custom prior encoding prior information # "Moderate" variance with SD around 1.0 suggests half-Cauchy(0, 1) # or exponential(1) for the SD custom_prior <- c(   set_prior("cauchy(0, 1)", class = "sd", group = "Person"),   set_prior("cauchy(0, 2)", class = "sd", group = "Task"),   set_prior("cauchy(0, 2)", class = "sd", group = "Rater:Task"),   set_prior("cauchy(0, 2)", class = "sd", group = "Person:Task"),   set_prior("cauchy(0, 2)", class = "sigma") )  # b) Fit with custom priors g_custom <- gstudy(   Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) +     (1 | Person:Task),   data = brennan,   backend = "brms",   prior = custom_prior )  # Compare g_bayes$variance_components$estimate g_custom$variance_components$estimate  # c) Sensitivity analysis scales <- c(0.5, 1, 2, 5) results <- lapply(scales, function(s) {   prior_s <- set_prior(paste0("cauchy(0, ", s, ")"), class = "sd")   g_s <- gstudy(     Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task),     data = brennan,     backend = "brms",     prior = prior_s   )   g_s$variance_components$estimate[1]  # Person variance })

previous_headings: Exercises > Computational Exercises
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Fit Bayesian G-study brennan data. Compute D-study coefficients designs 2-5 tasks 1-3 raters. Create table showing designs achieve G > 0.80 95% probability. Visualize relationship design reliability.
code: # a) G-study g_ex3 <- gstudy(   Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater:Task) +     (1 | Person:Task),   data = brennan,   backend = "brms",   chains = 4,   iter = 4000 )  # b) D-study sweep d_ex3 <- dstudy(   g_ex3,   n = list(Task = 2:5, `Rater:Task` = 1:3),   ci = c("g", "phi") )  # c) Designs achieving G > 0.80 library(dplyr) d_ex3$coefficients %>%   filter(g_lower > 0.80) %>%   arrange(g_lower)  # d) Visualization plot(d_ex3, type = "sweep")

previous_headings: Exercises > Computational Exercises
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Using rajaratnam dataset: Fit multivariate Bayesian G-study items nested within subtests. Compute VAR subtest 95% credible intervals. subtests reported separately? Justify using VAR results.
code: data(rajaratnam)  # a) Multivariate G-study g_mv_ex4 <- gstudy(   bf(Score ~ 0 + Subtest +          (0 + Subtest | r | Person) +          (0 + Subtest || Item)),   sigma ~ 0 + Subtest,   data = rajaratnam,   backend = "brms",   chains = 2,   iter = 2000 )  # b) VAR with credible intervals d_mv_ex4 <- dstudy(   g_mv_ex4,   n = list(Person = 5),   ci = c("var_rel", "var_abs") )  prmse(d_mv_ex4)  # c) Interpretation # VAR > 1 indicates subscale adds value # VAR < 1 indicates composite is better # VAR $\approx$ 1 means either approach acceptable

previous_headings: Exercises > Computational Exercises
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: Fit three models increasingly informative priors variance components. Compare posterior distributions variance components across models. Discuss prior sensitivity matters .
code: # a) Three prior specifications prior_weak <- set_prior("cauchy(0, 5)", class = "sd") prior_mod <- set_prior("cauchy(0, 1)", class = "sd") prior_strong <- set_prior("exponential(2)", class = "sd")  g_weak <- gstudy(Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task),                  data = brennan, backend = "brms", prior = prior_weak) g_mod <- gstudy(Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task),                 data = brennan, backend = "brms", prior = prior_mod) g_strong <- gstudy(Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task),                    data = brennan, backend = "brms", prior = prior_strong)  # b) Compare posteriors library(ggplot2) draws_weak <- as_draws_df(g_weak$model) draws_mod <- as_draws_df(g_mod$model) draws_strong <- as_draws_df(g_strong$model)  # c) Prior sensitivity matters most when: # - Sample size is small # - Variance components are near zero # - Priors strongly conflict with data

previous_headings: Technical Appendix: Bayesian Computation
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: brms compiles models Stan. simple person ×\times item design:
code: // Translated to Stan pseudocode data {   int<lower=0> N;           // Observations   int<lower=0> J;           // Persons   int<lower=0> K;           // Items   array[N] int<lower=1,upper=J> person;   array[N] int<lower=1,upper=K> item;   vector[N] score; }  parameters {   real mu;                  // Grand mean   vector[J] z_p;            // Person random effects (std normal)   vector[K] z_i;            // Item random effects (std normal)   real<lower=0> sigma_p;    // Person SD   real<lower=0> sigma_i;    // Item SD   real<lower=0> sigma_e;    // Residual SD }  model {   // Priors   mu ~ normal(0, 10);   sigma_p ~ cauchy(0, 2);   // Half-Cauchy   sigma_i ~ cauchy(0, 2);   sigma_e ~ cauchy(0, 2);    // Random effects   z_p ~ std_normal();   z_i ~ std_normal();    // Likelihood   score ~ normal(mu + sigma_p * z_p[person] + sigma_i * z_i[item], sigma_e); }  generated quantities {   // Variance components (on variance scale)   real var_p = square(sigma_p);   real var_i = square(sigma_i);   real var_e = square(sigma_e); }

previous_headings: Technical Appendix: Bayesian Computation
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: models small variance components, brms uses non-centered parameterization: τp=στ⋅zp,zp∼N(0,1)\tau_p = \sigma_\tau \cdot z_p, \quad z_p \sim N(0, 1) avoids posterior geometries cause slow sampling στ≈0\sigma_\tau \approx 0. “funnel doom” — non-centered parameterization matters. variance component στ\sigma_\tau near zero, centered parameterization τp=στ⋅zp\tau_p = \sigma_\tau \cdot z_p creates pathological posterior geometry known Neal’s funnel. στ→0\sigma_\tau \0, distribution τp\tau_p becomes extremely narrow: particular draw zpz_p gets multiplied tiny στ\sigma_\tau, τp\tau_p squeezed toward zero. result curved, narrow region high probability opens like funnel στ\sigma_\tau grows. HMC’s leapfrog integrator, assumes roughly Euclidean geometry, struggles navigate funnel — proposals land low-probability regions get rejected, leading slow mixing divergent transitions. non-centered parameterization decouples geometry: instead τp=στ⋅zp\tau_p = \sigma_\tau \cdot z_p zp∼N(0,1)z_p \sim N(0, 1) στ\sigma_\tau prior, sample τp∼N(0,1)\tau_p \sim N(0, 1) directly let στ\sigma_\tau act scale. posterior becomes much closer Gaussian, HMC samples efficiently. brms automatically uses non-centered parameterization data suggest needed (small variance components, near-singular fits); can force manually setting bf(y ~ ..., autoscale = FALSE) specifying model form separates location scale. diagnostic watch : see divergent transitions brms output (printed “divergent transitions warmup”), usually indicates centered parameterization wrong choice non-centered help.

previous_headings: Technical Appendix: Bayesian Computation
title: Using facet for Generalizability Theory
text: warmup, NUTS adapts: Step size ϵ\epsilon: Target acceptance rate ~0.8 Diagonal mass matrix: Estimated sample variance Dense mass matrix: Optional, correlated posteriors Adaptation typically requires 500-1000 iterations.

text: George Michaelides https://research-portal.uea.ac.uk/en/persons/george-michaelides/ Norwich Business School, University East Anglia Duncan Jackson https://www.kcl.ac.uk/people/duncan-jackson/ Kings Business School, Kings College London

title: facet: framework for Generalizability Theory
text: facet provides comprehensive framework conducting Generalizability Theory (G-theory) analyses using variance component models. provides bridge classical test theory standard linear mixed-effects models, offering: Multiple backends: Frequentist approaches using mom (Method Moments/ANOVA) lme4 (Restricted Maximum Likelihood); Bayesian approachs using brms (NUTS / Hamiltonian Monte Carlo) Univariate multivariate analyses using mvbind() Built-datasets: Includes classic G-theory datasets like brennan rajaratnam Rich Visualization: Built-support variance plots, D-study sweeps, random effects caterpillar plots

title: facet: framework for Generalizability Theory
code: # Install from GitHub # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("michaelides/facet")

previous_headings: Usage
title: facet: framework for Generalizability Theory
text: G-study estimates variance components facet measurement design. facet package uses standard lme4 formula syntax:
code: library(facet)  # Load included Brennan dataset (10 persons, 3 tasks, 12 raters) data(brennan)  # Conduct a G-study with Persons, Tasks, and Raters g <- gstudy(Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater) +              (1 | Person:Task) + (1 | Person:Rater),              data = brennan)  # View variance components summary(g)

previous_headings: Usage
title: facet: framework for Generalizability Theory
text: D-study computes reliability coefficients specific measurement designs:
code: # Conduct a D-study with 5 tasks and 2 raters d <- dstudy(g, n = list(Task = 5, Rater = 2))  # View Generalizability (G) and Dependability (D) coefficients summary(d)

previous_headings: Usage
title: facet: framework for Generalizability Theory
text: Rapidly evaluate reliability across multiple sample size combinations:
code: # Explore different numbers of Tasks and Raters d_sweep <- dstudy(g, n = list(Task = c(2, 5, 10), Rater = c(1, 2, 5)))  # Plot the results plot(d_sweep, type = "sweep")

previous_headings: Usage
title: facet: framework for Generalizability Theory
text: Visualize random effects uncertainty using caterpillar plots:
code: # Plot random effects for Persons plot_ranef(g, which = "Person")

previous_headings: Features
title: facet: framework for Generalizability Theory
text: Automatic backend selection — intelligently chooses lme4, brms, method moments based formula structure Univariate multivariate G-studies — analyze multiple outcomes simultaneously using mvbind() Support Complex Designs — full support crossed, nested, mixed designs Sample size exploration — rapidly evaluate reliability across multiple designs using simple list-based input Advanced D-study options — support custom universe scores, error specifications, score aggregation

previous_headings: Features
title: facet: framework for Generalizability Theory
text: Confidence/credible intervals — backends provide interval estimates: brms offers Bayesian credible intervals, lme4/mom provide bootstrap analytical intervals Flexible display scales — view variance components variances standard deviations using scale argument Proportion variance — component expressed percentage total variance easy interpretation

what: Brennan's (2001) Generalizability Theory Example Data — brennan
title: Brennan's (2001) Generalizability Theory Example Data — brennan
text: classic dataset Brennan (2001) used illustrate Person crossed Task Rater-nested-within-Task design. design equation \(p \times t \times (r:t)\): Person crossed Task, Task panel 4 Raters. Raters 1–4 see Task 1, raters 5–8 see Task 2, raters 9–12 see Task 3. Rater nested within Task (crossed), random-effects formula must use (1 | Rater:Task) rather (1 | Rater). 120 rows correspond 10 persons x 3 tasks x 4 raters-per-task.

title: Brennan's (2001) Generalizability Theory Example Data — brennan

title: Brennan's (2001) Generalizability Theory Example Data — brennan

title: Calculate G and D Coefficients — calculate_coefficients

title: Calculate G and D Coefficients — calculate_coefficients
text: vc D-study variance components tibble. n Named list sample sizes facet (required aggregation). object Specification object measurement. Can : NULL (default): uses first component vc character string: "p" character vector: c("p", "p:d") formula: obj ~ p + p:d error Specification error components. Can : NULL (default): components object specification character string: "p:" character vector: c("p:", "p:r") formula: ~ p:+ p:r Note: facet specified object error, error raised. aggregation Character vector facets aggregate . Components containing facets divided sample size. Default NULL (aggregation). Note: facet specified object aggregation, error raised. residual_is Character string specifying facets make residual. example, "p::r" means residual represents p::r interaction. Default NULL (residual rescaled).

title: Calculate G and D Coefficients — calculate_coefficients
text: data frame columns: uni Universe score variance. Represents true individual differences trait measured. reflects systematic variance attributable object measurement (e.g., persons) accounting measurement error. classical test theory terms, corresponds "true score" variance. larger universe score variance indicates greater heterogeneity among objects measurement, generally improves precision relative comparisons. sigma2_delta Relative error variance. Error variance affecting relative decisions (rankings). Includes variance components interact object measurement (.e., components contain object facet). example, person x rater design person object, includes person:rater residual components. sigma2_delta_abs Absolute error variance. Error variance affecting absolute decisions. Includes variance components except object measurement. example, person x rater design person object, includes rater, person:rater, residual components. g Generalizability coefficient. Quantifies reliability measurements relative decisions—situations focus rank-ordering comparing objects measurement (e.g., identifying high vs. low performers). Calculated : g = uni / (uni + sigma2_delta). G coefficient ranges 0 1, higher values indicating better reliability. Values >= 0.70 typically considered acceptable group-level decisions, >= 0.80 preferred individual-level decisions. phi Dependability coefficient (Phi coefficient). Quantifies reliability measurements absolute decisions—situations focus absolute level measurement (e.g., determining score exceeds cutoff). Calculated : phi = uni / (uni + sigma2_delta_abs). Phi coefficient always <= G coefficient absolute error includes error sources, just affecting relative rankings. sem_rel Standard error measurement relative decisions. Calculated sqrt(sigma2_delta). Indicates typical error interpreting relative standing rank-order among objects measurement. sem_abs Standard error measurement absolute decisions. Calculated sqrt(sigma2_delta_abs). Indicates typical error interpreting absolute score levels criterion-referenced standards.

what: Compute VAR Using Haberman Formula (Per Draw) — compute_var_haberman_draws
title: Compute VAR Using Haberman Formula (Per Draw) — compute_var_haberman_draws
text: Computes Value Added Ratio (VAR) subscale using Haberman (2008) formula, evaluates whether subscale's scores better estimate universe score composite .

title: Compute VAR Using Haberman Formula (Per Draw) — compute_var_haberman_draws
code: compute_var_haberman_draws(   uni_cov_draws,   total_rel_cov_draws,   total_abs_cov_draws,   dimensions,   weights )

title: Compute VAR Using Haberman Formula (Per Draw) — compute_var_haberman_draws
text: uni_cov_draws 3D array (n_draws x n_dims x n_dims) universe score covariances. total_rel_cov_draws 3D array (n_draws x n_dims x n_dims) total observed covariances relative error. total_abs_cov_draws 3D array (n_draws x n_dims x n_dims) total observed covariances absolute error. dimensions Character vector dimension names. weights Named numeric vector weights composite scores.

title: Compute VAR Using Haberman Formula (Per Draw) — compute_var_haberman_draws
text: list containing: prmse_s_rel, prmse_s_abs Univariate subscale reliabilities. prmse_c_rel, prmse_c_abs PRMSE projecting weighted composite. prmse_p_rel, prmse_p_abs PRMSE projecting entire profile (diagonal slice). var_rel, var_abs Value Added Ratios (PRMSE_S / PRMSE_C). prmse_mv_rel, prmse_mv_abs Global multivariate PRMSE (trace formula).

title: Compute VAR Using Haberman Formula (Per Draw) — compute_var_haberman_draws
text: formula : $$VAR(S_i) = \frac{PRMSE(S_i)}{PRMSE(C \rightarrow S_i)}$$ PRMSE(C -> S_i) Haberman (2008) correct form: $$PRMSE(C \rightarrow S_i) = \frac{[\mathrm{Cov}(\tau_i, C)]^2}{\mathrm{Var}(\tau_i) \cdot \mathrm{Rel}(C) \cdot \mathrm{Var}(C)}$$ \(\mathrm{Cov}(\tau_i, C) = \sum_j w_j \, \mathrm{Cov}(\tau_i, \tau_j)\) computed universe-score covariance matrix. corrected form Vispoel et al. (2023) labeled "Equation 38" (printed equation contains typesetting error denominator; see prmse details).

title: Compute VAR Using Haberman Formula (Per Draw) — compute_var_haberman_draws
text: Haberman, S. J. (2008). can subscores value? Journal Educational Behavioral Statistics, 33(2), 204-229. Vispoel, W. P., Lee, H., Hong, H., & Chen, T. (2023). Applying multivariate generalizability theory psychological assessments. Psychological Methods, 28(4), 847-870.

title: Detect brms Estimation Issues — detect_brms_issues

code: detect_brms_issues(model, vc = NULL)

title: Detect brms Estimation Issues — detect_brms_issues
text: list components: high_rhat Logical indicating R-hat > 1.05 detected high_rhat_components Character vector components high R-hat low_bulk_ess Logical indicating low Bulk ESS detected low_bulk_ess_components Character vector components low Bulk ESS low_tail_ess Logical indicating low Tail ESS detected low_tail_ess_components Character vector components low Tail ESS treedepth_exceeded Logical indicating max treedepth exceeded treedepth_count Number iterations hit max treedepth

title: D-Study Helper Functions — dstudy-helpers
text: Internal helper functions extracted dstudy() maintainability. function handles distinct phase D-study setup computation.

title: Conduct a Decision (D) Study — dstudy
text: Compute generalizability dependability coefficients based results G-study. Allows exploring different measurement designs modifying number levels facet.

title: Conduct a Decision (D) Study — dstudy
code: dstudy(   gstudy_obj,   n = list(),   universe = NULL,   error = NULL,   aggregation = NULL,   residual_is = NULL,   estimation = NULL,   cut_score = NULL,   ci = NULL,   probs = c(0.025, 0.975),   weights = NULL,   ... )

title: Conduct a Decision (D) Study — dstudy
text: gstudy_obj object class "gstudy" gstudy(). n named list specifying number levels facet D-study design (e.g., list(items = 10, raters = 3)). Can also list vectors explore multiple sample sizes. multivariate G-studies fit unbalanced = TRUE, may instead tibble columns dim, facet, n, giving different sample size per (dimension, facet). Supplying multiple rows (dimension, facet) triggers sweep, case sweep performed independently within dimension. universe Specification components contribute universe score. object measurement (first component G-study formula) always included universe. Can : NULL (default): universe includes object measurement character string: "person:item" (object automatically added) character vector: c("person", "person:item") (object included) formula: ~ person + person:item Non-object components universe estimated unscaled (G-study) variance components. example, object "p" universe includes "p:o", universe score variance computed : var(p) + var(p:o). error Specification error components. Can : NULL (default): components universe (aggregation) character string: "person:item" character vector: c("person:item", "person:rater") formula: ~ person:item + person:rater Note: component specified universe error, error raised. Note: component specified aggregation error, warning issued component removed error (interaction terms preserved). aggregation Character vector facets aggregate . Components containing facets divided sample size. example, aggregation = "item" divides item person:item n_item. aggregation specified, main effects aggregation facets automatically excluded error variance (interaction terms kept). Default NULL (aggregation). residual_is Character string specifying facets make residual. example, "person:item:rater" means residual represents three-way interaction. Required aggregation specified want residual rescaled. Default NULL (residual rescaled). estimation Character string specifying calculate coefficients: "simple": uses point estimates variance components (lme4/mom backends) "posterior": uses full posterior distributions (brms backend) brms backend, posterior estimation always used ensure consistency variance component estimates coefficient calculations. avoids Jensen's inequality bias occur variance estimates computed mean(SD)^2 rather mean(SD^2). estimation = "posterior" requested non-brms backend, warning issued gstudy model refit backend = "brms". cut_score Optional numeric value specifying cutoff score criterion-referenced decisions. provided, calculates phi-cut coefficient (phi_cut) addition standard phi coefficient. multivariate models, can single value applied dimensions. Default NULL (phi-cut calculation). ci Character vector specifying coefficients compute credible intervals . Options: "g", "phi", "phi-cut". Can specify multiple: ci = c("g", "phi"). Credible intervals available using brms backend. Default NULL (credible intervals computed). probs Numeric vector length 2 specifying quantiles credible interval calculation. Default c(0.025, 0.975) 95% credible interval. Works like quantile() function. used ci specified. weights Numeric vector weights computing composite coefficients. Length must match number dimensions multivariate design. Default NULL, uses equal weights (1 dimension). applicable multivariate G-studies (mgstudy objects). ... Additional arguments (currently unused).

title: Conduct a Decision (D) Study — dstudy
text: object class "dstudy" containing: gstudy original G-study object variance_components tibble variance components D-study columns: component: Name variance component var_unscaled: Unscaled variance estimate (G-study) pct_unscaled: Percentage total unscaled variance var_scaled: Scaled variance (divided D-study sample sizes) pct_scaled: Percentage total scaled variance dim: Dimension/response variable (multivariate models) multivariate models posterior estimation, variance_components includes additional rows dim = "Composite" showing weighted composite variance component type, computed full posterior propagation. coefficients tibble G D coefficients. multivariate designs, coefficients tibble includes additional row dim = "Composite" showing weighted composite G Phi coefficients. n number levels facet object object measurement (first component G-study) universe universe components specification error error specification (provided) aggregation aggregation specification (provided) residual_is residual specification (provided) residual_composition facets make residual (parse_residual_facets) estimation estimation method used ("simple" "posterior") posterior List posterior distributions (estimation = "posterior") composite_posterior Posterior draws composite variance components downstream analysis (multivariate models posterior estimation) cut_score cutoff score used phi-cut calculation (provided) mu_y grand mean(s) used phi-cut calculation (cut_score provided) ci credible interval specification (provided) probs probability levels used credible interval calculation (ci provided)

title: Conduct a Decision (D) Study — dstudy
text: default, universe score variance contains object measurement (e.g., Person), remaining components contribute error variance. Use universe include additional components universe score (e.g., facet fixed applied setting). Use error restrict components count error (e.g., facets considered fixed source measurement error inference). Use aggregation measurement procedure involves averaging facet (e.g., averaging across multiple raters), variance components facet divided respective sample sizes computing coefficients.

title: Conduct a Decision (D) Study — dstudy
text: multivariate G-studies (mgstudy objects), composite coefficients provide overall reliability estimate weighted sum dimension scores. composite score : Y_composite = sum(w_d * Y_d) variance composite variance component computed : sigma^2_composite = w' * Sigma * w : w vector weights (one per dimension) Sigma variance-covariance matrix component Expanded, equals: sigma^2_composite = sum(w_d^2 * sigma^2_d) + 2 * sum(w_d * w_d' * sigma_dd') covariances (-diagonal elements) contribute dimensions correlated. means composite reliability often exceeds average dimension-specific reliabilities, composite "borrows strength" correlations. weights parameter controls contribution dimension: Default: NULL uses equal weights (1 dimension) Custom: Provide numeric vector length matching number dimensions posterior estimation brms backend, composite coefficients computed posterior draw, properly propagating uncertainty final estimates.

title: Conduct a Decision (D) Study — dstudy
code: # First conduct a G-study using the brennan dataset # (Person crossed with Task and Rater) g <- gstudy(Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater) +   (1 | Person:Task) + (1 | Person:Rater) + (1 | Task:Rater), data = brennan) #> Error: Interaction term(s) have as many or more levels than observations, making them confounded with the residual: #>  - Person:Rater: 120 levels (120 observations) #>  #> This occurs when an interaction has a unique combination for each observation, meaning the interaction variance cannot be distinguished from residual variance. #>  #> Solutions: #>  1. Remove the problematic interaction term from your model #>  2. Use method = "mom" for ANOVA-based estimation which handles this differently #>  3. Use backend = "brms" for Bayesian estimation #>  #> Note: In a fully crossed design where all facets are crossed with each other, the highest-order interaction is typically confounded with the residual and should not be included.  # D-study with specific sample sizes d <- dstudy(g, n = list(Task = 3, Rater = 4)) #> Error: object 'g' not found print(d) #> Error: object 'd' not found  # D-study exploring multiple sample sizes (sweep) d_sweep <- dstudy(g, n = list(Task = c(3, 5, 10), Rater = c(2, 4, 8))) #> Error: object 'g' not found print(d_sweep) #> Error: object 'd_sweep' not found  # D-study with aggregation (averaging over Raters) d_agg <- dstudy(g, n = list(Task = 3, Rater = 4),   aggregation = "Rater",   residual_is = "Person:Task:Rater") #> Error: object 'g' not found  # \donttest{ # D-study with posterior estimation (requires brms backend) g_brms <- gstudy(Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater),   data = brennan, backend = "brms") #> Compiling Stan what: facet: facet: framework for Generalizability Theory — facet-package
title: facet: facet: framework for Generalizability Theory — facet-package
text: Conduct generalizability theory analyses using variance component models estimated various methods (including ANOVA method moments, REML lme4, NUTS brms). Provides functions G-studies (estimating variance components) D-studies (computing generalizability dependability coefficients).

title: facet: facet: framework for Generalizability Theory — facet-package
text: facet package provides tools conducting generalizability theory analyses using variance component models. supports multiple backends including lme4 frequentist analyses brms Bayesian analyses. Key functions: gstudy(): Conduct G-study estimate variance components dstudy(): Conduct D-study compute reliability coefficients

title: Conduct a Generalizability (G) Study — gstudy
text: Estimate variance components generalizability theory analysis using mixed effects models. Supports frequentist (lme4), Bayesian (brms), method moments (mom) backends.

title: Conduct a Generalizability (G) Study — gstudy
code: gstudy(   formula,   data,   backend = c("auto", "lme4", "brms", "mom"),   facets = NULL,   nested = NULL,   unbalanced = FALSE,   ci_method = c("none", "profile", "boot"),   nsim = 1000,   boot.type = c("perc", "basic", "norm"),   prior = NULL,   ... )

title: Conduct a Generalizability (G) Study — gstudy
text: formula formula specifying G-study model. left-hand side outcome variable, right-hand side specify variance components using lme4-style syntax (e.g., y ~ (1|person) + (1|item)). multivariate models, use brms syntax: mvbind(y1, y2) ~ (1|person). data data frame containing variables formula. backend Character string specifying backend use. One "auto" (default, chooses based formula type), "lme4", "brms", "mom". "mom" uses method moments (ANOVA-based) variance component estimation. facets Character vector facet names (optional, auto-detected formula NULL). nested Optional named list specifying nesting relationships. Names nested facets values nesting facets. example, list(task = "rater") means task nested within rater. NULL (default), nesting auto-detected data structure. unbalanced Logical indicating whether enable unbalanced multivariate estimation. Default FALSE. TRUE: mom backend: dimension analyzed available data using Henderson's Method III variance component estimation. Correlations computed using pairwise complete cases. brms backend: implemented. Use long-format specification bf(Score ~ 0 + Dimension + (0+Dimension|Facet), sigma ~ 0 + Dimension) sparse multivariate data. lme4 backend: applicable (lme4 support multivariate models). ci_method Character string specifying method confidence intervals lme4 backend. One "none" (default, CIs), "profile" (accurate, slower), "boot" (bootstrap, accurate, slowest). applicable lme4 backend; brms mom provide CIs automatically. nsim Integer: number bootstrap simulations (ci_method = "boot"). Default 1000. boot.type Character: bootstrap type, "perc" (percentile), "basic", "norm" (normal-theory) (ci_method = "boot"). Default "perc". prior brmsprior object list priors created set_prior() related functions. applicable using brms backend. Use default_prior() see available parameters priors. ... Additional arguments passed backend fitting function (e.g., lmer() brm()) confint.merMod confidence intervals.

title: Conduct a Generalizability (G) Study — gstudy
text: object class "gstudy" containing: model fitted model object backend variance_components tibble estimated variance components facets Character vector facet names facet_n Named numeric vector sample sizes main effect facet sample_size_info Comprehensive sample size information including main effects, interactions, residual, nested effects backend backend used fitting is_multivariate Logical indicating model multivariate is_unbalanced Logical indicating multivariate model fit unbalanced = TRUE. set multivariate models. TRUE, per-dimension totals stored n_per_dim dstudy() accept per-dimension n tibble. n_per_dim Named list per-dimension observation counts (populated multivariate models fit unbalanced = TRUE). sample_size_info_per_dim Named list per-dimension sample size information (populated multivariate models fit unbalanced = TRUE). element list keyed facet/component, mirroring structure sample_size_info. formula formula used data original data n_obs Number observations

title: Conduct a Generalizability (G) Study — gstudy
code: # Basic univariate G-study with lme4 (default) g <- gstudy(Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater) +   (1 | Person:Task) + (1 | Person:Rater) + (1 | Task:Rater), data = brennan ) #> Error: Interaction term(s) have as many or more levels than observations, making them confounded with the residual: #>  - Person:Rater: 120 levels (120 observations) #>  #> This occurs when an interaction has a unique combination for each observation, meaning the interaction variance cannot be distinguished from residual variance. #>  #> Solutions: #>  1. Remove the problematic interaction term from your model #>  2. Use method = "mom" for ANOVA-based estimation which handles this differently #>  3. Use backend = "brms" for Bayesian estimation #>  #> Note: In a fully crossed design where all facets are crossed with each other, the highest-order interaction is typically confounded with the residual and should not be included.  # G-study with profile confidence intervals if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ g_prof <- gstudy(Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task),   data = brennan,   ci_method = "profile" ) } # }  # Method of moments G-study (ANOVA-based) g_mom <- gstudy(Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task) + (1 | Rater) +   (1 | Person:Task) + (1 | Person:Rater) + (1 | Task:Rater), data = brennan, backend = "mom" ) #> Warning: Error() model is singular  # \donttest{ # Bayesian G-study with brms g_bayes <- gstudy(Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Task),   data = brennan,   backend = "brms" ) #> what: Extract Posterior Draws from gstudy or dstudy Objects — gt_draws
title: Extract Posterior Draws from gstudy or dstudy Objects — gt_draws
text: Extracts pre-calculated posterior draws gstudy dstudy objects returns wide-format tibble variance component coefficient separate column.

title: Extract Posterior Draws from gstudy or dstudy Objects — gt_draws
code: gt_draws(object, ...)  # S3 method for class 'gstudy' gt_draws(object, components = NULL, dims = NULL, n = NULL, ...)  # S3 method for class 'mgstudy' gt_draws(object, components = NULL, dims = NULL, n = NULL, ...)  # S3 method for class 'dstudy' gt_draws(   object,   what = c("all", "coefficients", "composite", "var", "variance"),   coefficients = NULL,   dims = NULL,   ... )

title: Extract Posterior Draws from gstudy or dstudy Objects — gt_draws
text: object gstudy dstudy object ... Additional arguments passed methods components Character vector variance components extract. NULL (default), components extracted. dims Character vector dimensions extract (multivariate models). NULL (default), dimensions extracted. n Named list sample sizes D-study calculation. NULL (default), sample sizes extracted G-study data. Character string specifying extract: "" Extract available draws (default) "coefficients" Extract coefficient draws (uni, sigma2_delta, g, phi, etc.) "composite" Extract composite posterior draws (multivariate ) "var" Extract VAR PRMSE draws (multivariate ) "variance" Extract variance component draws underlying gstudy coefficients Character vector coefficients extract. NULL (default), coefficients extracted.

title: Extract Posterior Draws from gstudy or dstudy Objects — gt_draws
text: tibble named list tibbles posterior draws wide format univariate gstudy objects, tibble columns: draw: Integer draw index Variance component columns (e.g., person, item, Residual) Coefficient columns: uni, sigma2_delta, sigma2_delta_abs, g, phi, sem_rel, sem_abs multivariate gstudy objects (mgstudy), named list tibbles, one per dimension, column structure univariate. univariate dstudy objects, tibble columns: draw: Integer draw index Coefficient columns: uni, sigma2_delta, sigma2_delta_abs, g, phi, sem_rel, sem_abs multivariate dstudy objects, named list tibbles: One tibble per dimension (coefficients VAR columns merged) $composite: Composite posterior draws (available)

title: Extract Posterior Draws from gstudy or dstudy Objects — gt_draws
text: gstudy objects, function extracts: Variance component draws random effect residual Derived coefficients (uni, sigma2_delta, g, phi, etc.) calculated using sample sizes original G-study provided via n dstudy objects, function extracts pre-calculated posterior draws computed estimation = "posterior" specified dstudy() call. multivariate dstudy objects, VAR/PRMSE columns merged coefficient columns dimension's tibble.

title: Extract Posterior Draws from gstudy or dstudy Objects — gt_draws
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Univariate gstudy g <- gstudy(score ~ (1 | person) + (1 | item), data = my_data, backend = "brms") draws <- gt_draws(g)  # Filter specific components draws <- gt_draws(g, components = c("person", "Residual"))  # Multivariate gstudy - returns named list mg <- gstudy(cbind(score1, score2) ~ (1 | person) + (1 | item),   data = my_data, backend = "brms") draws <- gt_draws(mg) draws$score1  # Access dimension-specific draws draws$score2  # Filter to specific dimensions draws <- gt_draws(mg, dims = "score1") } # } if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # D-study with posterior estimation g <- gstudy(score ~ (1 | person) + (1 | item), data = my_data, backend = "brms") d <- dstudy(g, n = list(item = 10), estimation = "posterior")  # Extract all draws draws <- gt_draws(d)  # Extract only coefficient draws draws <- gt_draws(d, what = "coefficients")  # Multivariate - returns named list mg <- gstudy(cbind(score1, score2) ~ (1 | person) + (1 | item),   data = my_data, backend = "brms") md <- dstudy(mg, n = list(item = 10), estimation = "posterior") draws <- gt_draws(md) draws$score1  # Per-dimension draws with VAR columns draws$composite  # Composite draws } # }

title: Parse Residual Facets from Formula — parse_residual_facets

title: Parse Residual Facets from Formula — parse_residual_facets
text: data provided, function analyzes actual data structure determine facets appearing interaction terms (like :d) nested crossed: Nested: level appears one level d, nested d. nested facet absorbed residual excludes nesting facet (e.g., residual = "p:" instead "p::d"). Crossed: levels appear multiple levels d, facets crossed. residual includes facets (e.g., "p::d").

title: Parse Residual Facets from Formula — parse_residual_facets
code: # Fully crossed design: p, i, d all crossed with each other f1 <- score ~ (1 | p) + (1 | i) + (1 | d) parse_residual_facets(f1) # Returns "p:i:d" #> Error in parse_residual_facets(f1): could not find function "parse_residual_facets"  # With nested data: i nested in d nested_data <- data.frame(   score = rnorm(100),   p = factor(rep(1:10, 10)),   i = factor(rep(1:5, each = 20)), # i levels repeat within d   d = factor(rep(1:2, each = 50)) ) f2 <- score ~ (1 | p) + (1 | i:d) + (1 | d) parse_residual_facets(f2, nested_data) # May return "p:i" if i nested in d #> Error in parse_residual_facets(f2, nested_data): could not find function "parse_residual_facets"
