what: Tidy PRMSE and VAR Results — prmse
title: Tidy PRMSE and VAR Results — prmse
text: Extract PRMSE(C→S_i) VAR results data frame dstudy object. univariate models, returns G Phi coefficients PRMSE metrics.

title: Tidy PRMSE and VAR Results — prmse
code: prmse(   dstudy_obj,   include_composite = FALSE,   include_profile = TRUE,   ci = NULL,   probs = c(0.025, 0.975),   weights = NULL,   optimize = NULL,   optimize_target = "rel",   grid_resolution = 0.1,   subscale = NULL )

title: Tidy PRMSE and VAR Results — prmse
text: dstudy_obj dstudy object dstudy() include_composite Logical. TRUE, adds Composite row summary metrics. Default FALSE. Composite row shows composite reliability (G/Phi) prmse_s_rel/abs columns, prmse_c_rel/abs var_rel/abs set 1.0 (trivially, composite perfectly predicts ). include_profile Logical. TRUE (default), includes prmse_p_rel prmse_p_abs columns showing well entire profile predicts subscale. FALSE, columns omitted compact output. ci Character vector specifying metrics compute CIs . Options: "prmse", "var", . Default NULL (CIs). Note: Credible intervals available brms backend posterior estimation. mom backend, parameter ignored warning. probs Numeric vector length 2 specifying quantile probabilities credible intervals (brms backend ). Default c(0.025, 0.975) 95% CI. weights Numeric vector weights computing composite coefficients. Length must match number dimensions. Default NULL uses weights dstudy. alternative weights provided, calculations performed using posterior draws properly propagate uncertainty. optimize Character string specifying optimization method. One : "composite" Maximize composite reliability via generalized eigenvalue "subscale" Maximize VAR subscales (per-subscale minimax) "tuning" Grid search guided VAR diagnostics Default NULL (optimization). optimize_target Character string specifying VAR optimize. Either "rel" (G-based) "abs" (Phi-based). Default "rel". grid_resolution Numeric step size grid search "tuning" method. Must 0.01 0.5. Default 0.1. subscale Character string specifying target subscale "subscale" method. NULL (default), returns per-subscale results plus minimax.

title: Tidy PRMSE and VAR Results — prmse
text: univariate models, tibble : dim response variable name prmse_rel G coefficient (relative reliability) prmse_abs Phi coefficient (absolute reliability) prmse_rel_LL, prmse_rel_UL, prmse_abs_LL, prmse_abs_UL Confidence intervals (ci specified) multivariate models, tibble one row per dimension : dim Dimension/subscale name prmse_s_rel, prmse_s_abs Univariate subscale reliabilities (G Phi coefficients). baseline measures ignore information dimensions. prmse_c_rel, prmse_c_abs PRMSE predicting subscale true scores weighted composite. Used subscale viability analysis: PRMSE_S > PRMSE_C, subscale provides unique information beyond composite. prmse_p_rel, prmse_p_abs PRMSE projecting entire profile (dimensions). Can exceed PRMSE_S dimensions correlated borrowing predictive information related dimensions. included include_profile = TRUE (default). var_rel, var_abs Value Added Ratio (PRMSE_S / PRMSE_C). ..._LL, ..._UL Confidence interval limits requested metrics. include_composite = TRUE, Composite row added : prmse_s_rel/abs: Composite reliability (G/Phi coefficients) prmse_c_rel/abs: 1.0 (composite trivially predicts ) prmse_p_rel/abs: Multivariate reliability (include_profile = TRUE) var_rel/abs: 1.0 optimize specified, returns list optimization results containing optimal weights, eigenvalue/value, recomputed metrics.

title: Tidy PRMSE and VAR Results — prmse
text: univariate models, PRMSE_rel PRMSE_abs correspond directly G Phi coefficients, respectively: prmse_rel = G coefficient (relative decision reliability) prmse_abs = Phi coefficient (absolute decision reliability) metrics labeled differently maintain consistency multivariate output structure.

what: Multivariate Models: Three Types of PRMSE
title: Tidy PRMSE and VAR Results — prmse
text: multivariate models, three types PRMSE metrics computed, answering different question prediction accuracy:

title: Tidy PRMSE and VAR Results — prmse
text: univariate G coefficient dimension. measures well subscale's observed scores predict true scores, ignoring information dimensions. $$\text{PRMSE}_S = G = \frac{\sigma^2_{\text{universe}}}{\sigma^2_{\text{universe}} + \sigma^2_{\text{relative error}}}$$ baseline reliability dimension considered isolation.

title: Tidy PRMSE and VAR Results — prmse
text: well weighted composite score predicts subscale's true scores. Haberman (2008) formula: $$\mathrm{PRMSE}(C \S_i) = \frac{[\mathrm{Cov}(\tau_i, C)]^2}       {\mathrm{Var}(\tau_i) \cdot \mathrm{Rel}(C) \cdot \mathrm{Var}(C)}$$ \(\mathrm{Cov}(\tau_i, C) = \sum_j w_j \, \mathrm{Cov}(\tau_i, \tau_j)\) weighted covariance subscale \(\)'s true score composite true score, computed universe-score (disattenuated) covariance matrix, observed-score sample covariances. \(\mathrm{Var}(\tau_i) = \Sigma_{\tau,ii}\) universe-score variance subscale \(\). \(\mathrm{Rel}(C) = \mathrm{Var}(\tau_C) / \mathrm{Var}(C)\) composite reliability (G coefficient relative, \(\Phi\) absolute). \(\mathrm{Var}(C) = \mathbf{w}^\top \Sigma_{\mathrm{obs}} \mathbf{w}\) D-study observed variance composite (scaled decision study sample sizes). Note Vispoel et al. (2023) Equation 38: Vispoel et al. (2023, p. 9) label quantity "Equation 38", printed equation contains typesetting error (denominator uses \(\hat\sigma^2(S_i) \cdot \mathrm{Rel}(C)\) \(\mathrm{Var}(\tau_i) \cdot \mathrm{Rel}(C)\)). implementation uses corrected Haberman (2008) form, matches numerical results reported Vispoel et al.'s Table 8. Viability Analysis: Subscale viability supported \(\text{PRMSE}_S > \text{PRMSE}_C\), meaning subscale predicts true scores better composite . Value Added Ratio (VAR) formalizes : \(\text{VAR} = \text{PRMSE}_S / \text{PRMSE}_C\).

title: Tidy PRMSE and VAR Results — prmse
text: well entire profile (dimensions simultaneously) predicts subscale's true scores. uses multivariate structure potentially improve prediction accuracy borrowing information correlated dimensions: $$\text{PRMSE}_P[d] = \frac{[\Sigma_\tau \Sigma_{\text{obs}}^{-1} \Sigma_\tau]_{dd}}{\Sigma_{\tau,dd}}$$ \(\Sigma_\tau\) universe-score covariance matrix \(\Sigma_{\text{obs}}\) D-study observed covariance matrix. Diagonal slice vs. trace: prmse_p_rel returns diagonal slice matrix \(\Sigma_\tau \Sigma_{\text{obs}}^{-1} \Sigma_\tau\) (one value per dimension). companion attribute attr(result, "prmse_mv_rel") returns trace matrix, normalized trace \(\Sigma_\tau\), single global multivariate profile reliability: $$\text{PRMSE}_{MV} = \frac{\mathrm{tr}(\Sigma_\tau \Sigma_{\text{obs}}^{-1} \Sigma_\tau)}{\mathrm{tr}(\Sigma_\tau)}$$ trace equals matrix analog squared canonical correlation always satisfies \(\text{PRMSE}_{MV} \geq \text{PRMSE}_P[d]\) dimension \(d\) (Cauchy-Schwarz Cholesky factor \(\Sigma_{\text{obs}}^{-1/2}\Sigma_\tau\)). Relationship PRMSE_S: dimensions uncorrelated (-diagonal covariances = 0), matrices become diagonal PRMSE_P simplifies PRMSE_S. case, prmse_p_rel approximately equal prmse_s_rel. metrics differ meaningfully : Dimensions correlated correlations substantial enough provide predictive value large difference PRMSE_P PRMSE_S indicates multivariate structure provides additional predictive information beyond dimension offers alone. can occur one dimension high reliability correlated less reliable dimension, allowing profile "borrow strength" improved prediction.

title: Tidy PRMSE and VAR Results — prmse
text: include_composite = TRUE, Composite row added output. row summarizes composite score's reliability: prmse_s_rel/abs: composite's G Phi coefficients prmse_c_rel/abs: Always 1.0 (composite perfectly predicts ) prmse_p_rel/abs: Multivariate reliability (include_profile = TRUE) var_rel/abs: Always 1.0

title: Tidy PRMSE and VAR Results — prmse
text: three optimization methods address fundamental tension multivariate generalizability theory: "composite": Finds weights maximize composite reliability (PRMSE). Uses analytical solution via generalized eigenvalue decomposition. Best selection/norm-referencing decisions. "subscale": Finds weights maximize VAR subscales. Returns per-subscale optimizations minimax optimization. Best profile interpretation subscales matter. "tuning": Exhaustive grid search weight combinations. Useful diagnostic purposes understanding weight-VAR relationships.

title: Tidy PRMSE and VAR Results — prmse
text: Credible intervals available using brms backend posterior estimation. computed directly posterior draws variance components PRMSE metrics. mom backend (method moments), credible intervals available method produces point estimates without posterior distribution. credible intervals needed, consider using brms backend:
code: g_mv <- gstudy(formula, data, backend = "brms") d_mv <- dstudy(g_mv, n = ...) prmse(d_mv, ci = "prmse") # CIs from posterior draws

title: Tidy PRMSE and VAR Results — prmse
text: function experimental. Testing limited, results verified cited published work.

title: Tidy PRMSE and VAR Results — prmse
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Univariate model g_uni <- gstudy(Score ~ (1 | Person) + (1 | Item), data = mydata) d_uni <- dstudy(g_uni, n = list(Item = 5)) prmse(d_uni) # Returns G and Phi as prmse_rel and prmse_abs  # Multivariate model with mom backend (no CIs available) g_mv <- gstudy(Score ~ 0 + Subtest + (0 + Subtest | Person),   data = data, dimension_var = "Subtest", backend = "mom") d_mv <- dstudy(g_mv, n = list(Person = 5)) prmse(d_mv)  # Include Composite row prmse(d_mv, include_composite = TRUE)  # Exclude profile projection metrics for compact output prmse(d_mv, include_profile = FALSE)  # Understanding PRMSE_S vs PRMSE_P: # When dimensions are uncorrelated, prmse_p_rel ≈ prmse_s_rel # When correlated, prmse_p_rel may exceed prmse_s_rel result <- prmse(d_mv) diff <- result$prmse_s_rel - result$prmse_p_rel if (all(abs(diff) < 0.01)) {   message("Dimensions appear uncorrelated: PRMSE_P ≈ PRMSE_S") } else {   message("Dimensions are correlated: PRMSE_P differs from PRMSE_S") }  # Weight optimization prmse(d_mv, optimize = "composite") prmse(d_mv, optimize = "subscale")  # Multivariate model with brms backend (credible intervals available) library(brms) g_mv <- gstudy(   bf(Score ~ 0 + Subtest + (0 + Subtest | r | Person)),   data = data, backend = "brms" ) d_mv <- dstudy(g_mv, n = list(Person = 5)) prmse(d_mv, ci = "prmse") # Returns point estimates with credible intervals } # }

what: Simulate Data for Generalizability Theory Analysis — simulate_gtheory_data
title: Simulate Data for Generalizability Theory Analysis — simulate_gtheory_data
text: Generate simulated data univariate multivariate generalizability theory (G-theory) analyses. Supports wide-format long-format multivariate designs, optional correlation structures residual random effects.

title: Simulate Data for Generalizability Theory Analysis — simulate_gtheory_data
code: simulate_gtheory_data(   facets = NULL,   formula = NULL,   vc = NULL,   sd_residual = 1,   multivariate = FALSE,   n_dims = 2,   dim_names = NULL,   format = c("wide", "long"),   dim_var = "dimension",   response_var = NULL,   residual_cor = NULL,   re_cor = NULL,   nested = NULL,   seed = NULL,   prefix = "" )

title: Simulate Data for Generalizability Theory Analysis — simulate_gtheory_data
text: facets named list specifying number levels facet. example, list(person = 50, item = 10) creates crossed design 50 persons 10 items. Names valid R variable names. formula optional lme4-style formula derive facet structure. provided, overrides facets. formula use random effects syntax, e.g., y ~ (1|person) + (1|item). vc named list variance components (variances, SDs). Names match facet names interactions (use : interactions). example, list(person = 1, item = 0.5, "person:item" = 0.3). sd_residual Standard deviation residual error term. Default 1. multivariate Logical. TRUE, generates multivariate data. Default FALSE. n_dims Number dimensions (outcome variables) multivariate data. Default 2. Ignored multivariate = FALSE. dim_names Character vector dimension names. NULL, dimensions named y1, y2, etc. wide format. format Character string specifying output format: "wide" "long". Wide format multiple outcome columns; long format dimension indicator variable. Default "wide". dim_var Name dimension indicator variable long format. Default "dimension". response_var Name response variable. Default "y" univariate "score" multivariate long format. residual_cor correlation matrix residual errors across dimensions. Must symmetric positive-definite matrix dimensions matching n_dims. NULL (default) implies independent residuals (identity matrix). re_cor named list correlation matrices random effects. Names match facet names. matrix symmetric positive-definite dimensions matching n_dims. NULL (default) implies independent random effects. nested named list specifying nesting relationships. Names nested facets, values facets nested within. example, list(item = "person") means items nested within persons. seed Optional random seed reproducibility. prefix Prefix factor level names. Default empty string, producing levels like "1", "2", etc.

title: Simulate Data for Generalizability Theory Analysis — simulate_gtheory_data
text: data frame : Columns facet (factors) One outcome columns (univariate: y, wide multivariate: y1, y2, ..., long multivariate: score dimension column) long format: dimension indicator column

title: Simulate Data for Generalizability Theory Analysis — simulate_gtheory_data
text: Variance components can specified using either vc parameter individual sd_* parameters. vc parameter takes named list names correspond facet names interactions (use : separate).

title: Simulate Data for Generalizability Theory Analysis — simulate_gtheory_data
text: univariate designs, function generates data according : $$y_{ijk...} = \mu + \epsilon_p + \epsilon_i + \epsilon_{pi} + \epsilon_{residual}$$ \(\epsilon\) term drawn normal distribution specified variance component.

title: Simulate Data for Generalizability Theory Analysis — simulate_gtheory_data
text: multivariate designs, correlations dimensions can specified: Residual correlations: Correlations residual errors across dimensions Random effect correlations: Correlations random effects specific facet Correlations implemented using Cholesky decomposition ensure proper multivariate normal distributions.

title: Simulate Data for Generalizability Theory Analysis — simulate_gtheory_data
text: Wide format: Multiple outcome columns (y1, y2, ...) - suitable mvbind() syntax gstudy() Long format: Single outcome column dimension indicator - suitable brms long-format models like score ~ 0 + dimension + (0+dimension|facet)

title: Simulate Data for Generalizability Theory Analysis — simulate_gtheory_data
code: # Univariate p x i design data_uni <- simulate_gtheory_data(   facets = list(person = 20, item = 10),   vc = list(person = 1, item = 0.5, "person:item" = 0.3),   sd_residual = 1 )  # Wide-format multivariate (2 dimensions) data_wide <- simulate_gtheory_data(   facets = list(person = 50, item = 10),   vc = list(person = 1, item = 0.5),   multivariate = TRUE,   n_dims = 2,   format = "wide" )  # Long-format multivariate with correlations cor_matrix <- matrix(c(1, 0.5, 0.5, 1), nrow = 2) data_long <- simulate_gtheory_data(   facets = list(person = 50, item = 10),   vc = list(person = 1, item = 0.5),   multivariate = TRUE,   n_dims = 2,   format = "long",   residual_cor = cor_matrix )

what: Variance Component Extraction and Manipulation — variance-components

what: Weight Optimization for Composite Scores — viable-optimization
title: Weight Optimization for Composite Scores — viable-optimization
text: Functions finding optimal weights composite scores multivariate generalizability theory. Three optimization methods provided: "composite": Maximize composite reliability (PRMSE) via generalized eigenvalue "subscale": Maximize VAR subscales (per-subscale minimax) "tuning": Grid search guided VAR diagnostics
